### 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (2024 Scheme):**
> In 14-mark questions, students *very often* lose 2–3 marks by:
> 1. ❌ Forgetting to mention the **loss function** used in supervised learning.
> 2. ❌ Confusing **classification accuracy** with **clustering metrics**.
> 3. ❌ Writing the **Bellman equation** without deriving it.
> 4. ❌ Failing to give **one real-world example per paradigm**.
>
> **Always include:** mathematical formulation + real example + diagram (where applicable).

---

#### **Question A (Choice 1) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the four main paradigms of machine learning with a clear block diagram. For each paradigm, state the type of data required, the goal, and one real-world application. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM) principle for supervised learning, starting from the expected risk definition. Show how the empirical risk is obtained and explain the role of the regularization term. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Four Paradigms Explanation (7 marks):**

| Paradigm | Data Type | Goal | Real-World Application |
|----------|-----------|------|------------------------|
| **Supervised** | Labeled $(X, y)$ | Learn $f: X \to y$ | Email spam detection |
| **Unsupervised** | Unlabeled $X$ | Discover hidden structure | Customer segmentation |
| **Semi-Supervised** | Few labels + many unlabeled | Improve accuracy with less labels | Web page classification |
| **Reinforcement** | $(S, A, R, S')$ trajectories | Maximize cumulative reward | Self-driving cars |

*(2 marks for the table; 3 marks for the diagram; 2 marks for clear prose explaining how each paradigm uses its feedback signal.)*

**Block Diagram:** Refer to Section 4.1 of these notes (4.1 Master Taxonomy of ML Paradigms).

**(b) ERM Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Expected Risk (1 mark):**
$$
R(f) = \mathbb{E}_{(x,y) \sim P(X,Y)}[\mathcal{L}(f(x), y)] = \int\int \mathcal{L}(f(x), y)\, dP(x,y)
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Approximation (2 marks):**
Replace unknown $P$ with the empirical distribution $\hat{P} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \delta(x-x_i)\delta(y-y_i)$:

$$
\hat{R}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i)
$$

**Step 3 — Add Regularizer (2 marks):**
$$
\mathcal{J}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i) + \lambda\Omega(f)
$$

**Step 4 — Final Optimization (2 marks):**
$$
f^{*} = \arg\min_{f \in \mathcal{F}}\left[\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i) + \lambda\Omega(f)\right]
$$

*Role of $\lambda\Omega(f)$:* It penalizes model complexity, preventing overfitting and improving generalization to unseen data.

---

#### **Question B (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the Reinforcement Learning paradigm. Define the key terms: agent, environment, state, action, reward, and policy. Formulate the Markov Decision Process (MDP) formally. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the Bellman Expectation Equation for the state-value function $V^{\pi}(s)$ starting from the definition of the discounted return $G_t$. Show clearly how the recursion $G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}$ leads to the final equation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Reinforcement Learning Explanation (7 marks):**

**Key Terms (2 marks — 0.25 each):**
- **Agent** — The learner / decision-maker (e.g., the robot).
- **Environment** — The external world the agent interacts with.
- **State $s$** — A representation of the current situation.
- **Action $a$** — A move the agent can make.
- **Reward $r$** — Scalar feedback signal from the environment.
- **Policy $\pi(a \vert s)$** — The agent's strategy mapping states to actions.

**MDP Formalization (3 marks):**
The MDP is the tuple $(\mathcal{S}, \mathcal{A}, P, R, \gamma)$:
- $\mathcal{S}$ — set of all states
- $\mathcal{A}$ — set of all actions
- $P(s' \vert s, a)$ — transition probability function
- $R(s, a)$ — reward function
- $\gamma \in [0, 1)$ — discount factor

**Diagram (2 marks):** Refer to Section 4.3 (Agent-Environment Loop).

**(b) Bellman Equation Derivation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Define Return (1 mark):**
$$
G_t = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}
$$

**Step 2 — Unroll Recursion (2 marks):**
$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma R_{t+2} + \gamma^{2} R_{t+3} + \cdots
$$
$$
G_t = R_{t+1} + \gamma (R_{t+2} + \gamma R_{t+3} + \cdots) = R_{t+1} + \gamma G_{t+1}
$$

**Step 3 — Take Expectation Conditioned on $S_t = s$ (2 marks):**
$$
\mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_t \mid S_t = s] = \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[R_{t+1} \mid S_t = s] + \gamma \, \mathbb{E}_{\pi}[G_{t+1} \mid S_t = s]
$$

**Step 4 — Apply Law of Total Expectation (2 marks):**
$$
V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) \big[ R(s, a, s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s') \big]
$$

This is the **Bellman Expectation Equation** — the foundation of all RL algorithms.

---

### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (High-Density Bullet Format):**

- ✅ **Machine Learning** = Learning from experience $E$ to improve performance $P$ on task $T$ (Mitchell's definition).
- ✅ **Four Paradigms:** Supervised, Unsupervised, Semi-Supervised, Reinforcement — distinguished by *feedback type*.
- ✅ **Supervised** needs labeled data; goal is to minimize $\frac{1}{N}\sum \mathcal{L}(f(x_i), y_i) + \lambda\Omega(f)$.
- ✅ **Unsupervised** has no labels; goal is structure discovery (clustering, DR, density).
- ✅ **Semi-Supervised** = small labeled + large unlabeled; governed by cluster / smoothness / manifold assumptions.
- ✅ **Reinforcement** = agent + environment + reward + policy; goal is to maximize $\mathbb{E}[G_t] = \mathbb{E}\left[\sum \gamma^{k} R_{t+k+1}\right]$.
- ✅ **Bellman Equation:** $V^{\pi}(s) = \sum_{a} \pi(a \vert s) \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) [R(s,a,s') + \gamma V^{\pi}(s')]$.
- ✅ **Bellman Optimality:** $V^{*}(s) = \max_{a} [R(s,a) + \gamma \sum_{s'} P(s' \vert s, a) V^{*}(s')]$.
- ✅ **Q-Learning Update:** $Q(s,a) \leftarrow Q(s,a) + \alpha[r + \gamma \max_{a'} Q(s',a') - Q(s,a)]$.
- ✅ **Key Loss Functions:** MSE for regression, Cross-Entropy for classification.
- ✅ **K-Means Optimal Centroid:** $\mu_k = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert} \sum_{x_i \in C_k} x_i$ (mean of cluster points).
- ✅ **PCA Objective:** Reconstruct original $x_i$ from low-dim projection $W W^{\top} x_i$.
- ✅ **Mnemonic to Remember Paradigms:** **"S U S R"** → **S**upervised, **U**nsupervised, **S**emi-supervised, **R**einforcement.
- ✅ **Examples to Memorize:**
  - Supervised → MNIST classification
  - Unsupervised → Customer segmentation
  - Semi-Supervised → Web page categorization
  - Reinforcement → AlphaGo, autonomous driving
- ✅ **Discount factor $\gamma \in [0, 1)$** controls how much future rewards matter vs. immediate rewards.
- ✅ **Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)** is the foundational principle of supervised learning.
- ✅ **Structural Risk Minimization (SRM)** = ERM + Regularization (Vapnik's principle).

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Day Tip:** Always draw a **labeled diagram** in 14-mark questions. Examiners in KTU 2024 Scheme award **1–2 bonus marks** for visual clarity and well-labeled figures even if the explanation is average. Use the diagrams in Section 4 as templates.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basics of Parameter Estimation: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori Estimation (MAP), Bayesian Formulation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT304 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basics of Parameter Estimation: Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori Estimation (MAP), Bayesian Formulation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Parameter Estimation?

In statistical learning and machine learning, every model is governed by **parameters** (often denoted as $\theta$) that control its behaviour. For example, the mean $\mu$ and variance $\sigma^2$ of a Gaussian distribution, the bias $b$ and weights $w$ of a linear regression model, or the success probability $p$ of a Bernoulli trial. **Parameter Estimation** is the formal statistical procedure of inferring the *most plausible values* of these unknown parameters from a finite set of observed data.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):** Parameter estimation is the process of using observed data to estimate the unknown parameters of a probability distribution or statistical model, typically by formulating an objective function (likelihood, posterior, or loss) and optimizing it.

There are two major philosophical schools within parameter estimation:

1. **Frequentist Approach (Frequentist Statistics)** – Assumes parameters are *fixed but unknown constants*. The data is treated as random, and the goal is to find a single "best" estimate. *Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)* is the canonical frequentist method.
2. **Bayesian Approach (Bayesian Statistics)** – Treats parameters as *random variables* themselves, with a prior belief about their values that is updated once data is observed. *Maximum A Posteriori Estimation (MAP)* and the full *Bayesian Formulation* are the two pillars of this approach.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy – The Weather Detective

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy: "The Weather Detective"**
> Imagine a detective trying to estimate the probability $p$ that it will rain tomorrow in Kochi during August.
> - **MLE viewpoint (Pure Data Detective):** He looks at the last 30 days of August weather data. He counts 27 rainy days out of 30 and concludes $\hat{p} = 0.9$. He uses *only the evidence*. This is the Maximum Likelihood Estimate.
> - **MAP viewpoint (Pragmatic Detective):** He knows from 50 years of climatology that August in Kochi has a prior belief of $p \approx 0.7$ with some confidence. He then combines this prior with the new 30-day data to get a *compromise estimate*, perhaps $\hat{p} \approx 0.82$. This is the MAP Estimate.
> - **Full Bayesian (Scholarly Detective):** Instead of giving a single number, he produces a *full probability distribution* over all possible values of $p$ — saying "there is a 60% chance $p$ is between 0.75 and 0.85, 30% chance above 0.85, and so on." This is the Bayesian Posterior.

## 1.3 Formal Definitions

### 1.3.1 Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)

Given a probability model $P(x \mid \theta)$ and an observed dataset $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ consisting of i.i.d. samples, the **Likelihood Function** is defined as:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid \theta)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE):** The value of the parameter $\theta$ that *maximizes* the likelihood function is called the Maximum Likelihood Estimate, denoted $\hat{\theta}_{MLE}$.

$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\; \mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$$

### 1.3.2 Maximum A Posteriori Estimation (MAP)

MAP extends MLE by incorporating a **prior distribution** $P(\theta)$ on the parameters, representing our belief about $\theta$ *before* observing the data. Using **Bayes' Theorem**, the **posterior distribution** is:

$$P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Maximum A Posteriori Estimate (MAP):** The value of $\theta$ that maximizes the *posterior* distribution is the MAP estimate.

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\; P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\; P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)$$

Since the evidence $P(\mathcal{D})$ is independent of $\theta$, it is dropped from the optimization.

### 1.3.3 Bayesian Formulation (Full Posterior Inference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bayesian Formulation:** Rather than collapsing the parameter to a single point estimate, the full Bayesian approach *retains the entire posterior distribution* $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ and uses it for prediction, decision-making, and uncertainty quantification via the predictive distribution:

$$P(x_{new} \mid \mathcal{D}) = \int P(x_{new} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) \, d\theta$$

This integral is the **posterior predictive distribution**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Bayesian Reasoning

The Bayesian paradigm rests on three foundational elements. Understanding the *role* of each is critical for KTU examination answers.

### 2.1.1 Prior Distribution $P(\theta)$ — *"What we believe before seeing data"*
- Encodes domain knowledge, expert belief, or historical data.
- Can be **informative** (sharp, peaked — strong belief) or **non-informative / flat** (e.g., uniform — weak belief).
- Choice of prior is the most *philosophically debated* aspect of Bayesian methods.

### 2.1.2 Likelihood $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ — *"How probable is the data under a given parameter value?"*
- Same as the MLE objective.
- Bridges data and parameters.

### 2.1.3 Posterior $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ — *"What we believe after seeing data"*
- The *updated* belief about $\theta$ after observing the evidence.
- Proportional to Likelihood $\times$ Prior (normalizing constant is the evidence).

## 2.2 Why Log-Likelihood? — Operational Simplification

Maximizing the product form of the likelihood directly is computationally problematic (numerical underflow for large $n$, and products are harder to differentiate than sums). Since $\log(\cdot)$ is a **monotonically increasing** function, maximizing the log-likelihood is *equivalent* to maximizing the likelihood.

$$\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\; \log \mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$$

Similarly for MAP:

$$\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \underset{\theta}{\arg\max}\; \left[ \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid \theta) + \log P(\theta) \right]$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU:** MAP = MLE + a regularization term. The negative log-prior acts as a *regularizer* that penalizes parameter values deemed improbable by the prior. This is why MAP is widely used in machine learning to prevent overfitting.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Remarks / Use-Case** |
|---|---|---|
| Likelihood Function | $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Assumes i.i.d. samples |
| Log-Likelihood | $\ell(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$ | Used for numerical stability |
| Bayes' Theorem | $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) \, P(\theta)}{P(\mathcal{D})}$ | Fundamental identity of Bayesian inference |
| MLE Objective | $\hat{\theta}_{MLE} = \arg\max_{\theta} \mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ | Pure data-driven estimate |
| MAP Objective | $\hat{\theta}_{MAP} = \arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$ | Data + prior compromise |
| Posterior Predictive | $P(x_{new} \mid \mathcal{D}) = \int P(x_{new} \mid \theta) P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) d\theta$ | Full Bayesian prediction |
| MLE $\to$ MAP Relation | $\log P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) + \log P(\theta) - \log P(\mathcal{D})$ | MAP adds $\log P(\theta)$ as regularizer |
| MLE of Bernoulli $p$ | $\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ | From $\frac{\partial \ell}{\partial p} = 0$ |
| MAP of Bernoulli (Beta prior) | $\hat{p}_{MAP} = \frac{\sum x_i + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}$ | With $p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$ |
| MLE of Gaussian $\mu$ | $\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ | Known variance case |
| MAP of Gaussian $\mu$ | $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \frac{\sigma^2 \mu_0 + n \tau^2 \bar{x}}{\sigma^2 + n \tau^2}$ | With $\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau^2)$ |

## 2.4 The MLE $\Leftrightarrow$ MAP Spectrum

- When the prior is **uniform / non-informative** $P(\theta) = \text{const}$, then MAP collapses to MLE.
- When $n \to \infty$ (massive data), the likelihood term dominates, and MAP also converges to MLE.
- When data is scarce, the prior in MAP has a *strong influence* on the estimate.
- Full Bayesian > MAP > MLE in terms of uncertainty handling, but also in computational cost.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Recognition (HMMs):** Emission probabilities are estimated using MLE on labelled audio corpora.
- **Medical Diagnosis:** MAP is used in Bayesian neural networks to incorporate prior medical knowledge when patient data is limited.
- **Spam Filters (Naive Bayes):** Token probabilities are estimated using smoothed MAP (Laplace / Dirichlet priors).
- **Autonomous Vehicles (Sensor Fusion):** Kalman filters are essentially running a recursive MAP estimate of vehicle state from noisy GPS + IMU data.
- **Climate Modelling:** Full Bayesian posteriors quantify uncertainty in future temperature predictions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Exam Strategy:** In KTU valuation, every intermediate differentiation step in an MLE/MAP derivation carries credit. Skipping steps is the most common reason students lose 2–3 marks per part. We write every transition explicitly.

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — MLE for Bernoulli Distribution

**Setup:** Let $\mathcal{D} = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}$ be i.i.d. samples from a Bernoulli distribution with unknown parameter $p$ (success probability), where $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

**Step 1: Write the probability mass function.**

$$P(x_i \mid p) = p^{x_i} (1-p)^{1-x_i}$$

**Step 2: Form the Likelihood Function (product over all samples).**

$$\mathcal{L}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p^{x_i} (1-p)^{1-x_i}$$

**Step 3: Rewrite as a single power expression.**

Since $\prod p^{x_i} = p^{\sum x_i}$ and $\prod (1-p)^{1-x_i} = (1-p)^{n - \sum x_i}$:

$$\mathcal{L}(p) = p^{\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i} \; (1-p)^{n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}$$

Let $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$ (number of successes). Then:

$$\mathcal{L}(p) = p^{S} \, (1-p)^{n-S}$$

**Step 4: Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood.**

$$\ell(p) = \log \mathcal{L}(p) = S \log p + (n - S) \log(1-p)$$

**Step 5: Differentiate with respect to $p$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{S}{p} - \frac{n - S}{1 - p} = 0$$

**Step 6: Solve the resulting algebraic equation for $p$.**

$$\frac{S}{p} = \frac{n - S}{1 - p}$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$S(1 - p) = (n - S) p$$

$$S - Sp = np - Sp$$

$$S = np$$

**Step 7: Final MLE solution.**

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{S}{n} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \bar{x}}$$

**Step 8: Second-derivative verification (concavity check for maximum).**

$$\frac{d^2 \ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{S}{p^2} - \frac{n - S}{(1-p)^2} < 0 \quad \forall\, p \in (0,1)$$

Since the second derivative is strictly negative, the critical point is indeed a *maximum*. ✓

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — MAP for Bernoulli with Beta Prior

**Setup:** Suppose a domain expert believes the Bernoulli parameter $p$ is well-modelled by a Beta prior: $p \sim \text{Beta}(\alpha, \beta)$, with PDF:

$$P(p) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} \, p^{\alpha - 1} (1-p)^{\beta - 1} \propto p^{\alpha - 1} (1-p)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 1: Form the unnormalized posterior using Bayes' theorem.**

$$P(p \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto P(\mathcal{D} \mid p) \, P(p) \propto p^{S} (1-p)^{n-S} \cdot p^{\alpha - 1} (1-p)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 2: Combine the exponents.**

$$P(p \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto p^{S + \alpha - 1} (1-p)^{(n - S) + \beta - 1}$$

**Step 3: Take the logarithm.**

$$\log P(p \mid \mathcal{D}) = (S + \alpha - 1) \log p + (n - S + \beta - 1) \log(1-p) + \text{const}$$

**Step 4: Differentiate with respect to $p$ and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d}{dp} \log P(p \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{S + \alpha - 1}{p} - \frac{n - S + \beta - 1}{1 - p} = 0$$

**Step 5: Solve for $p$.**

$$(S + \alpha - 1)(1 - p) = (n - S + \beta - 1) p$$

$$S + \alpha - 1 - p(S + \alpha - 1) = p(n - S + \beta - 1)$$

$$S + \alpha - 1 = p \left[ (S + \alpha - 1) + (n - S + \beta - 1) \right]$$

$$S + \alpha - 1 = p \left[ n + \alpha + \beta - 2 \right]$$

**Step 6: Final MAP solution.**

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{MAP} = \frac{S + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** This is the famous *add-one (Laplace) smoothing* formula when $\alpha = \beta = 2$ (or sometimes $\alpha = \beta = 1$ for the uniform prior case). It prevents zero probabilities in language models like spam filters, which is critical when a rare word never appeared in training.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — MLE and MAP for the Mean of a Gaussian

**Setup:** Let $x_1, \dots, x_n \overset{i.i.d.}{\sim} \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with known $\sigma^2$. We want estimates for $\mu$.

### 3.3.1 MLE for $\mu$

**Step 1: Write the Gaussian PDF.**

$$P(x_i \mid \mu) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi \sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 2: Form the log-likelihood.**

$$\ell(\mu) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left[ -\frac{1}{2} \log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right]$$

$$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{n}{2} \log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3: Differentiate and set to zero.**

$$\frac{d\ell}{d\mu} = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \cdot 2 \sum_{i=1}^{n} (\mu - x_i) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu) = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve.**

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i - n\mu = 0 \implies \boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i = \bar{x}}$$

### 3.3.2 MAP for $\mu$ with Gaussian Prior

**Setup:** Place a Gaussian prior on $\mu$: $\mu \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau^2)$.

**Step 1: Write the log-posterior.**

$$\log P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = \log P(\mathcal{D} \mid \mu) + \log P(\mu) + \text{const}$$

$$\log P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu)^2 - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\tau^2} + \text{const}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate with respect to $\mu$.**

$$\frac{d}{d\mu} \log P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n} (x_i - \mu) - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)}{\tau^2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Expand and collect terms in $\mu$.**

$$\frac{n \bar{x}}{\sigma^2} - \frac{n \mu}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\mu}{\tau^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{n \bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^2} = \mu \left( \frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\tau^2} \right)$$

**Step 4: Solve for $\mu$.**

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \frac{\frac{n \bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^2}}{\frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\tau^2}} = \frac{\sigma^2 \mu_0 + n \tau^2 \bar{x}}{\sigma^2 + n \tau^2}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** The MAP estimate is a *precision-weighted average* of the prior mean $\mu_0$ and the sample mean $\bar{x}$. When $n$ is large (data dominates), $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x}$ (MLE). When $\tau^2 \to \infty$ (vague prior), $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x}$ (MLE). This is the *single most exam-relevant result* in this module.

---

## 3.4 The MLE–MAP–Bayesian Comparison Matrix

| **Aspect** | **MLE** | **MAP** | **Full Bayesian** |
|---|---|---|---|
| Treats $\theta$ as | Fixed constant | Random variable | Random variable |
| Uses prior $P(\theta)$? | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes |
| Output | Single point $\hat{\theta}$ | Single point $\hat{\theta}$ | Full posterior $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ |
| Computational cost | Low | Low–Moderate | High (often requires MCMC/VI) |
| Uncertainty captured? | No (asymptotic only) | No | Yes |
| Equivalent to MLE when | — | Prior is uniform | Prior is uniform |
| Risk of overfitting | High | Lower (regularized) | Lowest |
| KTU 2024 frequency | ⭐⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐⭐ | ⭐⭐⭐ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Parameter Estimation Family Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA[Parameter Estimation] --> rootB[Frequentist Approach]
    rootA --> rootC[Bayesian Approach]
    
    rootB --> mleA[Maximum Likelihood Estimation MLE]
    mleA --> mleB[Optimizes Likelihood P of D given theta]
    mleA --> mleC[No Prior Knowledge Required]
    mleA --> mleD[Point Estimate Output]
    
    rootC --> mapA[Maximum A Posteriori MAP]
    rootC --> bayesA[Full Bayesian Formulation]
    
    mapA --> mapB[Optimizes Posterior P of theta given D]
    mapA --> mapC[Uses Prior P of theta]
    mapA --> mapD[Point Estimate Output]
    mapA --> mapE[Acts as Regularized MLE]
    
    bayesA --> bayesB[Computes Full Posterior Distribution]
    bayesA --> bayesC[Predictive via Posterior Integration]
    bayesA --> bayesD[Captures Parameter Uncertainty]
    
    mapA -.->|Uniform Prior| mleA
    bayesA -.->|Uniform Prior| mleA
    mapA -.->|Large Data Limit| mleA
```

## 4.2 MLE vs MAP — Operational Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph freqBlock[Frequentist Pipeline]
        dataIn1[Observed Data D] --> lklhoodFn[Likelihood Function P of D given theta]
        lklhoodFn --> opt1[argmax Likelihood]
        opt1 --> mleOut[theta MLE - Single Point Estimate]
    end
    
    subgraph mapBlock[MAP Pipeline]
        dataIn2[Observed Data D] --> lklhoodFn2[Likelihood P of D given theta]
        priorIn[Prior Belief P of theta] --> bayesComb[Bayes Multiplication]
        lklhoodFn2 --> bayesComb
        bayesComb --> opt2[argmax Posterior]
        opt2 --> mapOut[theta MAP - Regularized Point Estimate]
    end
    
    subgraph fullBayesBlock[Full Bayesian Pipeline]
        dataIn3[Observed Data D] --> lklhoodFn3[Likelihood P of D given theta]
        priorIn3[Prior P of theta] --> bayesComb3[Bayes Theorem Full]
        lklhoodFn3 --> bayesComb3
        bayesComb3 --> postDist[Full Posterior P of theta given D]
        postDist --> predInt[Posterior Predictive Integration]
        postDist --> uncOut[Uncertainty Quantification]
    end
```

## 4.3 The Estimation Pipeline — Stepwise Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA[Start: Define Model and Identify Unknown Parameters] --> step1[Step 1: Collect i.i.d. Sample Data D]
    step1 --> step2{Q2: Do You Have Prior Knowledge?}
    step2 -->|No| mlePath[Use MLE]
    step2 -->|Yes| mapPath[Choose a Prior Distribution]
    
    mlePath --> mleStep1[Form Likelihood L of theta]
    mleStep1 --> mleStep2[Take log-likelihood]
    mleStep2 --> mleStep3[Differentiate and equate to zero]
    mleStep3 --> mleStep4[Verify Second Derivative is Negative]
    mleStep4 --> mleRes[Output theta hat MLE]
    
    mapPath --> mapStep1[Form Posterior P of theta given D]
    mapStep1 --> mapStep2[Take log-posterior]
    mapStep2 --> mapStep3[Differentiate and equate to zero]
    mapStep3 --> mapStep4[Verify Second Derivative is Negative]
    mapStep4 --> mapRes[Output theta hat MAP]
    
    mapRes --> bayesDecide{Need Full Uncertainty?}
    bayesDecide -->|Yes| fullBayes[MCMC or Variational Inference]
    bayesDecide -->|No| stop1[End]
    fullBayes --> stop1
    mleRes --> stop1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

> Differentiate between Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE) and Maximum A Posteriori (MAP) estimation. State one advantage of MAP over MLE.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| **Aspect** | **MLE** | **MAP** |
|---|---|---|
| Philosophy | Frequentist | Bayesian |
| Uses prior $P(\theta)$? | No | Yes |
| Objective | $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ | $\arg\max_{\theta} P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta)$ |
| Output | Point estimate only | Point estimate only |

**Advantage of MAP:** MAP incorporates prior knowledge, which helps in low-data regimes and acts as a regularizer, reducing overfitting. When the prior is uniform, MAP reduces to MLE.

*(Allocation: 2 marks for the comparison table, 1 mark for the advantage.)*

---

### **Question A2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

> Define the **Likelihood Function** and explain why the **log-likelihood** is preferred in practice for computing MLE.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Likelihood Function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ is the joint probability of the observed data, viewed as a function of the parameters $\theta$:

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid \theta)$$

The **log-likelihood** $\ell(\theta) = \log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log P(x_i \mid \theta)$ is preferred for three reasons:

1. **Numerical Stability:** Products of many small probabilities underflow to zero; logarithms convert them to manageable sums.
2. **Mathematical Convenience:** Sums are easier to differentiate than products, especially when applying calculus-based optimization (chain rule, partial derivatives).
3. **Monotonicity Preservation:** $\log(\cdot)$ is a strictly increasing function, so $\arg\max \log \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \arg\max \mathcal{L}(\theta)$. The estimate is unchanged.

*(Allocation: 1 mark for the definition, 2 marks for the three reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Module Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT skip the logarithm step** when forming the log-likelihood. Examiners deduct 1 mark for every missing transition.
> - **Do NOT forget the second-derivative sign check** to confirm a maximum. This is a 1-mark item students frequently miss.
> - **Do NOT confuse the Beta prior parameters $(\alpha, \beta)$ with the model parameters.** Clearly label each variable.
> - **In MAP derivations, ALWAYS show the proportionality drop of $P(\mathcal{D})$** before taking the log. Examiners expect this step explicitly.

---

### **Question B-A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Derive the **Maximum Likelihood Estimate** for the parameter $p$ of a Bernoulli distribution, given $n$ i.i.d. observations $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n \in \{0, 1\}$. Verify that your estimate corresponds to a maximum using the second-derivative test. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Suppose the parameter $p$ is now assumed to follow a **Beta prior distribution** with parameters $\alpha$ and $\beta$. Derive the **MAP estimate** of $p$ and discuss how it reduces to the MLE in the limit of large $n$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Bernoulli PMF (0.5 Mark)**

$$P(x_i \mid p) = p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i}$$

**Step 2: Form Likelihood (1 Mark)**

$$\mathcal{L}(p) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} p^{x_i}(1-p)^{1-x_i} = p^{\sum x_i} (1-p)^{n - \sum x_i}$$

Let $S = \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$. Then $\mathcal{L}(p) = p^S (1-p)^{n-S}$.

**Step 3: Log-Likelihood (0.5 Mark)**

$$\ell(p) = S \log p + (n - S) \log(1-p)$$

**Step 4: First Derivative and Critical Point (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{d\ell}{dp} = \frac{S}{p} - \frac{n - S}{1 - p} = 0$$

Cross-multiplying: $S(1-p) = (n - S)p \Rightarrow S - Sp = np - Sp \Rightarrow S = np$.

$$\hat{p}_{MLE} = \frac{S}{n} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i$$

**Step 5: Second-Derivative Test (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{d^2 \ell}{dp^2} = -\frac{S}{p^2} - \frac{n - S}{(1-p)^2}$$

For $p \in (0,1)$ and $0 < S < n$, both terms are strictly negative, so $\frac{d^2 \ell}{dp^2} < 0$. Hence the critical point is a **strict maximum**. ✓

**Step 6: Final Answer (1 Mark)**

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{MLE} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}$$

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Beta Prior (1 Mark)**

$$P(p) = \frac{1}{B(\alpha, \beta)} p^{\alpha - 1} (1-p)^{\beta - 1} \propto p^{\alpha - 1} (1-p)^{\beta - 1}$$

**Step 2: Unnormalized Posterior (1 Mark)**

$$P(p \mid \mathcal{D}) \propto \mathcal{L}(p) \cdot P(p) \propto p^{S + \alpha - 1} (1-p)^{(n - S) + \beta - 1}$$

**Step 3: Log-Posterior and Derivative (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{d}{dp} \log P(p \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{S + \alpha - 1}{p} - \frac{n - S + \beta - 1}{1 - p} = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve for $p$ (2 Marks)**

$$(S + \alpha - 1)(1 - p) = (n - S + \beta - 1) p$$

$$S + \alpha - 1 = p \cdot (n + \alpha + \beta - 2)$$

$$\boxed{\hat{p}_{MAP} = \frac{S + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}}$$

**Step 5: Limiting Behaviour (1 Mark)**

As $n \to \infty$, with $S \approx np$:

$$\hat{p}_{MAP} = \frac{np + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2} \to \frac{np}{n} = p = \hat{p}_{MLE}$$

Thus MAP asymptotically converges to MLE when the dataset is large — a manifestation of the likelihood swamping the prior.

---

### **Question B-B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE CHOICE]**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2025 Model]**

**(a)** Consider i.i.d. samples $x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n$ drawn from a Gaussian distribution $\mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with known variance $\sigma^2$. **Derive the MLE of $\mu$** and state whether it is unbiased. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Now assume $\mu$ has a Gaussian prior $\mathcal{N}(\mu_0, \tau^2)$. **Derive the MAP estimate of $\mu$** and interpret the result as a precision-weighted compromise between the prior and the data. Discuss the special cases when $n \to \infty$ and when $\tau^2 \to \infty$. **(7 Marks)**

#### **Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Gaussian PDF (0.5 Mark)**

$$P(x_i \mid \mu) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2\pi\sigma^2}} \exp\left( -\frac{(x_i - \mu)^2}{2\sigma^2} \right)$$

**Step 2: Log-Likelihood (1 Mark)**

$$\ell(\mu) = -\frac{n}{2} \log(2\pi\sigma^2) - \frac{1}{2\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2$$

**Step 3: Differentiate (1.5 Marks)**

$$\frac{d\ell}{d\mu} = \frac{1}{\sigma^2} \sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}(n\bar{x} - n\mu) = 0$$

**Step 4: Solve (1.5 Marks)**

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i}$$

**Step 5: Unbiasedness Check (2.5 Marks)**

$$E[\hat{\mu}_{MLE}] = E\left[\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} x_i\right] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} E[x_i] = \frac{1}{n} \sum_{i=1}^{n} \mu = \mu$$

Since $E[\hat{\mu}_{MLE}] = \mu$, the MLE is **unbiased** for the Gaussian mean. ✓

---

#### **Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1: Log-Posterior Setup (1 Mark)**

$$\log P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = -\frac{1}{2\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu)^2 - \frac{(\mu - \mu_0)^2}{2\tau^2} + \text{const}$$

**Step 2: Differentiate (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{d}{d\mu}\log P(\mu \mid \mathcal{D}) = \frac{1}{\sigma^2}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(x_i - \mu) - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\tau^2} = 0$$

$$\frac{n\bar{x} - n\mu}{\sigma^2} - \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\tau^2} = 0$$

**Step 3: Solve (2 Marks)**

$$\frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^2} = \mu\left(\frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\tau^2}\right)$$

$$\boxed{\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \frac{\frac{n\bar{x}}{\sigma^2} + \frac{\mu_0}{\tau^2}}{\frac{n}{\sigma^2} + \frac{1}{\tau^2}} = \frac{\sigma^2 \mu_0 + n\tau^2 \bar{x}}{\sigma^2 + n\tau^2}}$$

**Step 4: Precision-Weighted Interpretation (1 Mark)**

Recognize $1/\sigma^2$ as the *data precision* and $1/\tau^2$ as the *prior precision*. The MAP estimate is the *precision-weighted average* of the prior mean and the sample mean.

**Step 5: Limiting Cases (1 Mark)**

- As $n \to \infty$: $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x}$ — the data overwhelms the prior.
- As $\tau^2 \to \infty$ (vague prior): $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \bar{x}$ — the prior becomes uninformative.
- As $n \to 0$ (no data): $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} \to \mu_0$ — the prior dominates completely.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Module 1: Parameter Estimation**

- ⭐ **Parameter Estimation** = inferring unknown model parameters $\theta$ from observed data $\mathcal{D}$.
- ⭐ **Three approaches:** MLE (frequentist), MAP (Bayesian point estimate), Full Bayesian (posterior distribution).
- ⭐ **Likelihood Function** $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(x_i \mid \theta)$ is the *data likelihood*, not a probability density over $\theta$.
- ⭐ **Log-likelihood** is used for *numerical stability* and *easier differentiation*; the argmax is unchanged.
- ⭐ **Bayes' Theorem:** $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) = P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta) P(\theta) / P(\mathcal{D})$. The denominator $P(\mathcal{D})$ is the *evidence* and is dropped during optimization.
- ⭐ **Prior $P(\theta)$** encodes pre-existing belief; **Posterior $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$** is the updated belief.
- ⭐ **MLE for Bernoulli** $p$: $\hat{p}_{MLE} = \bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum x_i$ — derived by setting $\frac{d\ell}{dp} = 0$.
- ⭐ **MAP for Bernoulli (Beta prior):** $\hat{p}_{MAP} = \frac{S + \alpha - 1}{n + \alpha + \beta - 2}$.
- ⭐ **MLE for Gaussian mean:** $\hat{\mu}_{MLE} = \bar{x}$ — *unbiased* estimator.
- ⭐ **MAP for Gaussian mean (Gaussian prior):** $\hat{\mu}_{MAP} = \frac{\sigma^2 \mu_0 + n\tau^2 \bar{x}}{\sigma^2 + n\tau^2}$ — precision-weighted average.
- ⭐ **MAP = MLE + Regularization** — the $\log P(\theta)$ term in the log-posterior penalizes implausible parameters, preventing overfitting.
- ⭐ **MLE is a special case of MAP** when the prior is uniform / non-informative, or in the limit of large $n$.
- ⭐ **Full Bayesian** integrates over the posterior to make predictions, capturing *uncertainty* in $\theta$.
- ⭐ **Posterior Predictive Distribution:** $P(x_{new} \mid \mathcal{D}) = \int P(x_{new} \mid \theta) P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D}) d\theta$.
- ⭐ **Always verify the second derivative** $\frac{d^2 \ell}{d\theta^2} < 0$ (or use the Hessian for vector parameters) to confirm a *maximum*.
- ⭐ **KTU Pitfall:** Never confuse $P(\mathcal{D} \mid \theta)$ (likelihood) with $P(\theta \mid \mathcal{D})$ (posterior). Examiners specifically test this.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Supervised Learning: Feature Representation and Problem Formulation, Role of Loss Functions and Optimization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT304 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Supervised Learning: Feature Representation and Problem Formulation, Role of Loss Functions and Optimization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Supervised Learning — The Formal Definition

**Supervised Learning** is a paradigm in Machine Learning (ML) wherein a learning algorithm is presented with a *labeled* training dataset consisting of input-output pairs, and the objective is to learn a mapping (function) from the input space to the output space such that the learned function generalizes well to previously unseen data.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme formal notation, a supervised learning problem is defined as:

- Let the **input space** be $\mathcal{X} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^d$ (a $d$-dimensional real-valued feature space).
- Let the **output space** (or label space) be $\mathcal{Y}$.
  - If $\mathcal{Y} \subseteq \mathbb{R}$, the task is **regression**.
  - If $\mathcal{Y} = \{1, 2, \ldots, K\}$ (discrete classes), the task is **classification**.
- Let the **training set** be $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$, drawn i.i.d. from an unknown joint distribution $P(X, Y)$.
- The learner selects a function $f_\theta : \mathcal{X} \to \mathcal{Y}$ from a **hypothesis class** $\mathcal{H}$, parameterized by $\theta \in \Theta$, such that a chosen **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}), y)$ is minimized on average.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Supervised learning is the *foundational pillar* of the entire course. Every subsequent module (linear models, trees, neural networks) builds upon the problem formulation, loss function design, and optimization strategy introduced here. Mastery of this module is non-negotiable for scoring above **80%** in Module 1.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are teaching a young child to recognize fruits.

You repeatedly show the child an apple and say, *"This is an apple."* You show a banana and say, *"This is a banana."* After many such examples, the child begins to identify fruits they have *never seen before* based on features like color, shape, and texture.

In this analogy:
- The **child** is the learning algorithm.
- The **(apple, "apple")** pairs are the **labeled training data**.
- The **internal rule** the child builds (e.g., *"round and red → apple"*) is the **hypothesis function** $f_\theta$.
- The child's **mistakes** (e.g., confusing a red apple with a tomato) are measured by the **loss function**.
- The **feedback** ("No, that is a tomato!") is the **optimization signal** that updates the child's internal rule.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Three Pillars of Supervised Learning:**
> 1. **Feature Representation** — *How do we describe the data numerically?*
> 2. **Problem Formulation** — *What function are we trying to learn?*
> 3. **Loss Function & Optimization** — *How do we measure and minimize error?*

---

## 1.3 Feature Representation — Core Idea

A **feature** is a measurable property or characteristic of a phenomenon being observed. A **feature vector** $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ is a $d$-dimensional numerical encoding of a single data instance.

**Examples of Features:**
- For a house: area (sq ft), number of bedrooms, age (years), location (latitude, longitude).
- For an email: word counts, presence of attachments, sender domain, length.

**Common Feature Representation Schemes:**

| Representation Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Raw Numerical** | Direct measurement values | $[180.5,\ 3,\ 25]$ (area, rooms, age) |
| **One-Hot Encoding** | Binary vector for categorical features | `Red` → $[1,0,0]$, `Green` → $[0,1,0]$ |
| **Bag-of-Words (BoW)** | Frequency-based text encoding | "cat dog" → $[1,1,0,\ldots]$ |
| **Scaling / Normalization** | Rescaling features to a common range | Min-Max: $x' = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ |
| **Standardization** | Zero mean, unit variance | $z = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Feature Representation Matters:** The principle *"garbage in, garbage out"* applies absolutely. A poorly represented feature space leads to poor model performance, regardless of algorithm sophistication. Feature engineering is often the single most impactful step in building ML systems.

---

## 1.4 Problem Formulation — Core Idea

The **problem formulation** translates a real-world question into a mathematical optimization problem. It involves:

1. **Defining the hypothesis class** $\mathcal{H}$ (e.g., all linear functions, all decision trees of depth $\leq 5$).
2. **Defining the loss function** $\mathcal{L}$ (e.g., squared error, cross-entropy).
3. **Defining the objective** — minimize the **expected risk**:

$$R(\theta) = \mathbb{E}_{(X,Y) \sim P} \left[ \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(X), Y) \right]$$

Since $P$ is unknown, we minimize the **empirical risk** (training error) as a proxy:

$$\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}\left(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)}\right)$$

---

## 1.5 Loss Functions — Core Idea

A **loss function** $\mathcal{L}(\hat{y}, y)$ quantifies the *discrepancy* between the prediction $\hat{y} = f_\theta(\mathbf{x})$ and the true label $y$. It is the *yardstick* by which the model's quality is measured.

> [!NOTE]
> **Loss Function vs. Cost Function:** In KTU notation, the **loss** is computed on a *single* example, while the **cost** (or empirical risk) is the *average* loss over the entire training set. Many textbooks use these terms interchangeably — be aware of the convention used in your exam.

---

## 1.6 Optimization — Core Idea

**Optimization** is the process of finding the parameters $\theta^*$ that minimize the loss function. The most fundamental algorithm is **Gradient Descent (GD)**, which iteratively updates parameters in the direction of steepest descent of the loss surface.

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t)})$$

where $\eta > 0$ is the **learning rate** (a hyperparameter) and $\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ is the gradient of the loss with respect to parameters.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Supervised Learning Pipeline — Structured Breakdown

The complete supervised learning workflow decomposes into six logical stages:

1. **Data Collection** — Gather labeled examples $\mathcal{D} = \{(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}, y^{(i)})\}_{i=1}^{N}$.
2. **Feature Representation** — Transform raw data into feature vectors $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^d$.
3. **Model Selection (Hypothesis Class)** — Choose $\mathcal{H}$ (linear, polynomial, tree-based, neural).
4. **Loss Function Design** — Choose $\mathcal{L}$ appropriate to the task (regression vs. classification).
5. **Optimization** — Solve $\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \hat{R}(\theta)$ using GD or its variants.
6. **Evaluation & Generalization** — Measure performance on a held-out test set.

---

## 2.2 Feature Representation — Detailed Theory

### 2.2.1 Feature Vector Formalism

Each training instance is a $d$-dimensional column vector:

$$\mathbf{x}^{(i)} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^{(i)} \\ x_2^{(i)} \\ \vdots \\ x_d^{(i)} \end{bmatrix} \in \mathbb{R}^d$$

The entire training set is stored as a **design matrix** $\mathbf{X} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times d}$:

$$\mathbf{X} = \begin{bmatrix} (\mathbf{x}^{(1)})^T \\ (\mathbf{x}^{(2)})^T \\ \vdots \\ (\mathbf{x}^{(N)})^T \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} x_1^{(1)} & x_2^{(1)} & \cdots & x_d^{(1)} \\ x_1^{(2)} & x_2^{(2)} & \cdots & x_d^{(2)} \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ x_1^{(N)} & x_2^{(N)} & \cdots & x_d^{(N)} \end{bmatrix}$$

The label vector is $\mathbf{y} = (y^{(1)}, y^{(2)}, \ldots, y^{(N)})^T \in \mathbb{R}^N$.

### 2.2.2 The "Why" Behind Feature Scaling

Features measured in different units (e.g., age in years vs. salary in rupees) have different magnitudes. This causes:
- **Gradient descent to oscillate** in directions of high-magnitude features.
- **Distance-based algorithms (KNN, SVM)** to be dominated by large-scale features.
- **Regularization** to unfairly penalize small-coefficient features.

**Standardization** (Z-score normalization) addresses this:

$$x_j^{\text{std}} = \frac{x_j - \mu_j}{\sigma_j}$$

where $\mu_j$ and $\sigma_j$ are the mean and standard deviation of feature $j$ computed on the training set.

### 2.2.3 One-Hot Encoding — Detailed

For a categorical feature with $K$ distinct values, one-hot encoding creates $K$ binary features. If the value is the $k$-th category, the vector has a 1 in position $k$ and 0 elsewhere.

**Example:** Color $\in \{\text{Red}, \text{Green}, \text{Blue}\}$ → $\text{Red} = [1,0,0]^T$, $\text{Green} = [0,1,0]^T$, $\text{Blue} = [0,0,1]^T$.

**Curse of Dimensionality:** When $K$ is very large (e.g., 100,000 unique words), one-hot vectors become sparse and high-dimensional. Dimensionality reduction (PCA) or embedding methods are used to mitigate this.

---

## 2.3 Problem Formulation — Detailed Theory

### 2.3.1 Hypothesis Class

The **hypothesis class** $\mathcal{H}$ defines the *space* of functions the learner is allowed to choose from. Examples:

| Hypothesis Class | Form | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Linear** | $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | Regression, linearly separable classification |
| **Polynomial (degree $p$)** | $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{j=0}^{p} w_j \phi_j(\mathbf{x})$ | Non-linear regression |
| **Logistic** | $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$ | Binary classification |
| **Decision Trees** | Piecewise constant functions | Mixed data types |
| **Neural Networks** | Composed nonlinear transformations | Complex patterns |

### 2.3.2 Empirical Risk Minimization (ERM)

The cornerstone principle of supervised learning. Since the true distribution $P(X,Y)$ is unknown, we minimize the **empirical risk** as an estimator of the true risk:

$$\theta^* = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \hat{R}(\theta) = \arg\min_{\theta \in \Theta} \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$$

**The Generalization Gap:** By the law of large numbers, $\hat{R}(\theta) \to R(\theta)$ as $N \to \infty$. For finite $N$, there is a gap — this is why we use a **validation set** to estimate generalization.

---

## 2.4 Loss Functions — Exhaustive Catalog

### 2.4.1 Regression Losses

**(a) Squared Error Loss (L2 Loss):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}}(\hat{y}, y) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$$

- **Properties:** Differentiable, penalizes large errors heavily (quadratic penalty), sensitive to outliers.
- **Use case:** When errors are Gaussian-distributed; default for linear regression.

**(b) Absolute Error Loss (L1 Loss):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{MAE}}(\hat{y}, y) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$$

- **Properties:** Robust to outliers, not differentiable at zero (requires subgradient methods).
- **Use case:** When outliers are present or errors follow a Laplace distribution.

**(c) Huber Loss** (combines L1 and L2):

$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{Huber}}(\hat{y}, y) = \begin{cases} \frac{1}{2}(y - \hat{y})^2 & \text{if } \vert y - \hat{y} \vert \leq \delta \\ \delta \cdot \left(\vert y - \hat{y} \vert - \frac{1}{2}\delta\right) & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}$$

- **Properties:** Quadratic for small errors, linear for large errors. Differentiable everywhere.

### 2.4.2 Classification Losses

**(a) 0-1 Loss (Theoretical Gold Standard):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{0\text{-}1}(\hat{y}, y) = \mathbb{1}[\hat{y} \neq y]$$

- **Properties:** Non-differentiable, non-convex. Used for theoretical analysis (e.g., Bayes risk), not for optimization.

**(b) Cross-Entropy Loss (Log Loss):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CE}}(\hat{y}, y) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{p}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

for binary classification where $\hat{p} = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$.

- **Properties:** Differentiable, convex for linear models, arises from MLE under Bernoulli likelihood.
- **Use case:** Logistic regression, neural network classifiers.

**(c) Hinge Loss (SVM):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}}(\hat{y}, y) = \max(0, 1 - y \cdot \hat{y})$$

where $y \in \{-1, +1\}$ and $\hat{y} = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$.

- **Properties:** Convex, encourages correct classification with margin, zero loss if prediction is correct with sufficient margin.

**(d) Categorical Cross-Entropy (Multi-class):**
$$\mathcal{L}_{\text{CCE}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k=1}^{K} y_k^{(i)} \log(\hat{p}_k^{(i)})$$

where $\hat{p}_k$ is the predicted probability for class $k$.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are KTU high-yield. Memorize and practice deriving each one from first principles.**

| # | Concept | Formula | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Empirical Risk | $\hat{R}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\mathcal{L}(f_\theta(\mathbf{x}^{(i)}), y^{(i)})$ | Average loss over training set |
| 2 | Linear Hypothesis | $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$ | $\theta = \{\mathbf{w}, b\}$ |
| 3 | MSE Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MSE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum(y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)})^2$ | Regression default |
| 4 | MAE Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{MAE}} = \frac{1}{N}\sum \vert y^{(i)} - \hat{y}^{(i)} \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 5 | Binary Cross-Entropy | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{BCE}} = -\frac{1}{N}\sum[y\log\hat{p} + (1-y)\log(1-\hat{p})]$ | Logistic regression |
| 6 | Hinge Loss | $\mathcal{L}_{\text{hinge}} = \max(0, 1 - y\hat{y})$ | SVM, $y \in \{-1, +1\}$ |
| 7 | Sigmoid | $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$ | Output of logistic regression |
| 8 | Softmax | $\sigma(\mathbf{z})_k = \frac{e^{z_k}}{\sum_{j}e^{z_j}}$ | Multi-class output |
| 9 | Standardization | $x^{\text{std}} = \frac{x - \mu}{\sigma}$ | Zero mean, unit variance |
| 10 | Min-Max Scaling | $x^{\text{norm}} = \frac{x - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}}$ | Range $[0, 1]$ |
| 11 | GD Update | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}$ | Learning rate $\eta > 0$ |
| 12 | SGD Update | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}^{(i)}$ | Uses single example |
| 13 | Mini-Batch GD | $\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}_{\text{batch}}$ | Batch size $B$ |
| 14 | Convergence (convex) | $\mathcal{O}(1/t)$ rate for GD | $t$ = iterations |
| 15 | Optimal $\eta$ (quadratic) | $\eta^* = \frac{1}{L}$ (Lipschitz constant) | For smooth convex losses |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Loss Function Used | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **House Price Prediction** | MSE | Continuous target, Gaussian noise assumption |
| **Fraud Detection** | Cross-Entropy (weighted) | Highly imbalanced classes |
| **Medical Diagnosis** | Hinge / CE | Need confident, margin-based decisions |
| **Stock Return Forecasting** | Huber | Outliers (crashes) are common |
| **Spam Classification** | BCE | Binary output, probabilistic interpretation |
| **Image Segmentation** | Dice / CCE | Pixel-wise classification |
| **Autonomous Driving** | MSE (regression on steering) | Continuous control output |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Gradient Descent for Linear Regression with MSE Loss

**Setup:** Hypothesis class: $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$, where $\mathbf{w} \in \mathbb{R}^d$ and $b \in \mathbb{R}$.

For simplicity, augment $\mathbf{x}$ with a constant 1: $\tilde{\mathbf{x}} = [1, \mathbf{x}^T]^T$ and $\theta = [b, \mathbf{w}^T]^T \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$, so $f_\theta(\mathbf{x}) = \theta^T \tilde{\mathbf{x}}$.

**Loss function (MSE):**
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left( y^{(i)} - \theta^T \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)} \right)^2$$

**Step 1: Compute the gradient of the loss with respect to $\theta$.**

We rewrite the loss compactly using matrix notation. Let $\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \in \mathbb{R}^{N \times (d+1)}$ be the augmented design matrix, and $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^N$ be the label vector.

The residual vector is:
$$\mathbf{e} = \mathbf{y} - \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta \in \mathbb{R}^N$$

So the loss becomes:
$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \mathbf{e}^T \mathbf{e} = \frac{1}{N} (\mathbf{y} - \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\theta)^T (\mathbf{y} - \tilde{\mathbf{X}}\theta)$$

**Step 2: Expand the quadratic form.**

$$\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ \mathbf{y}^T \mathbf{y} - 2 \mathbf{y}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta + \theta^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta \right]$$

**Step 3: Differentiate with respect to $\theta$.**

Using the standard rules: $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(\theta^T A \theta) = 2A\theta$ (when $A$ is symmetric) and $\frac{\partial}{\partial \theta}(b^T \theta) = b$:

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \left[ -2 \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \mathbf{y} + 2 \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta \right]$$

**Step 4: Simplify.**

$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{2}{N} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T (\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta - \mathbf{y})$$

**Step 5: Apply the gradient descent update rule.**

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \eta \cdot \nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta^{(t)})$$

$$\theta^{(t+1)} = \theta^{(t)} - \frac{2\eta}{N} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \left( \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta^{(t)} - \mathbf{y} \right)$$

**Step 6: Closed-form solution (Normal Equation).**

Setting the gradient to zero:
$$\frac{2}{N} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T (\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta^* - \mathbf{y}) = 0$$

$$\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta^* = \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \mathbf{y}$$

$$\theta^* = (\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}})^{-1} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \mathbf{y}$$

This is the **Normal Equation** — the closed-form solution for linear regression with MSE loss. It exists when $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ is invertible (i.e., features are linearly independent).

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Gradient of Cross-Entropy Loss for Logistic Regression

**Setup:** Hypothesis: $\hat{p} = \sigma(z) = \sigma(\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b)$, where $\sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$.

**Loss function (Binary Cross-Entropy):**
$$\mathcal{L}(\mathbf{w}, b) = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ y^{(i)} \log(\hat{p}^{(i)}) + (1 - y^{(i)}) \log(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \right]$$

**Step 1: Compute $\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{p}}$.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{p}} = -\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \left[ \frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{p}^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}} \right]$$

**Step 2: Compute $\frac{\partial \hat{p}}{\partial z}$ (sigmoid derivative).**

Let $\hat{p} = \sigma(z) = \frac{1}{1 + e^{-z}}$. Then:

$$\frac{d\hat{p}}{dz} = \sigma(z) \cdot (1 - \sigma(z)) = \hat{p}(1 - \hat{p})$$

**Step 3: Compute $\frac{\partial z}{\partial \mathbf{w}}$.**

Since $z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b$:
$$\frac{\partial z}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \mathbf{x}$$

**Step 4: Apply the chain rule.**

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = \frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial \hat{p}} \cdot \frac{\partial \hat{p}}{\partial z} \cdot \frac{\partial z}{\partial \mathbf{w}}$$

**Step 5: Simplify term by term for a single example.**

For a single example (drop the $\frac{1}{N}$ and the sum for clarity):

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}^{(i)}}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}}{\hat{p}^{(i)}} - \frac{1 - y^{(i)}}{1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}} \right] \cdot \hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

**Step 6: Simplify the bracket.**

$$-\left[ \frac{y^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)})\hat{p}^{(i)}}{\hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})} \right] \cdot \hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})$$

The $\hat{p}^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)})$ cancels:

$$= -\left[ y^{(i)}(1 - \hat{p}^{(i)}) - (1 - y^{(i)})\hat{p}^{(i)} \right] \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

$$= -\left[ y^{(i)} - y^{(i)}\hat{p}^{(i)} - \hat{p}^{(i)} + y^{(i)}\hat{p}^{(i)} \right] \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

$$= -\left[ y^{(i)} - \hat{p}^{(i)} \right] \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

$$= (\hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \cdot \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

**Step 7: Final gradient (full batch).**

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (\hat{p}^{(i)} - y^{(i)}) \mathbf{x}^{(i)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** Notice the elegant form of the gradient: it is simply the *prediction error* $(\hat{p} - y)$ times the *input* $\mathbf{x}$. This is why cross-entropy + sigmoid is such a popular combination — the gradient is clean and numerically stable, unlike mean-squared error with sigmoid (which suffers from the vanishing gradient problem).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Convexity Analysis of MSE Loss

**Claim:** The MSE loss $\mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} (y^{(i)} - \theta^T \tilde{\mathbf{x}}^{(i)})^2$ is a **convex function** of $\theta$.

**Proof:**

**Step 1:** A function $f$ is convex iff its Hessian $\nabla^2 f$ is positive semi-definite (PSD).

**Step 2:** Compute the Hessian of $\mathcal{L}$.

From Section 3.1 Step 4:
$$\nabla_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{2}{N} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T (\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \theta - \mathbf{y})$$

Differentiate again:
$$\nabla^2_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{2}{N} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$$

**Step 3:** Show $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ is PSD.

For any vector $\mathbf{v} \in \mathbb{R}^{d+1}$:
$$\mathbf{v}^T (\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}) \mathbf{v} = (\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{v})^T (\tilde{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{v}) = \Vert \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \mathbf{v} \Vert_2^2 \geq 0$$

Since the squared norm is always non-negative, $\tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}}$ is PSD.

**Step 4:** Conclusion.

$$\nabla^2_\theta \mathcal{L}(\theta) = \frac{2}{N} \tilde{\mathbf{X}}^T \tilde{\mathbf{X}} \succeq 0$$

Therefore, $\mathcal{L}(\theta)$ is convex. $\blacksquare$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convexity Implication:** For convex losses, gradient descent is *guaranteed* to converge to the global minimum. For non-convex losses (e.g., neural networks), GD can get stuck in local minima. This is a critical KTU high-yield concept.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Loss Function Properties

| Loss Function | Task Type | Convex? | Differentiable? | Robust to Outliers? | Probabilistic Origin |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MSE (L2)** | Regression | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Gaussian MLE |
| **MAE (L1)** | Regression | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (subgradient) | ✅ Yes | Laplace MLE |
| **Huber** | Regression | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes | ✅ Partially | Mixture MLE |
| **0-1 Loss** | Classification | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | Bayes risk |
| **Cross-Entropy** | Classification | ✅ Yes (linear) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Bernoulli MLE |
| **Hinge** | Classification | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (subgradient) | ✅ Yes | Max-margin |
| **Categorical CE** | Classification | ✅ Yes (linear) | ✅ Yes | ❌ No | Multinomial MLE |

---

## 3.5 Worked Example: One Step of Gradient Descent

**Problem:** Given data point $(\mathbf{x} = [2, 3]^T, y = 5)$ and current parameters $\mathbf{w} = [0.5, 0.8]^T, b = 0.1$, learning rate $\eta = 0.05$, perform one step of GD using MSE loss.

**Step 1: Compute prediction.**

$$z = \mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{x} + b = (0.5)(2) + (0.8)(3) + 0.1 = 1.0 + 2.4 + 0.1 = 3.5$$

$$\hat{y} = 3.5$$

**Step 2: Compute the prediction error.**

$$e = y - \hat{y} = 5 - 3.5 = 1.5$$

**Step 3: Compute the gradient of MSE with respect to $\mathbf{w}$.**

For a single example, $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = -2(y - \hat{y}) \mathbf{x} = -2(1.5)[2, 3]^T = [-6, -9]^T$

For $b$: $\nabla_b \mathcal{L} = -2(1.5) = -3$

**Step 4: Apply the update rule.**

$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{new}} = \mathbf{w} - \eta \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} \mathcal{L} = [0.5, 0.8]^T - (0.05)[-6, -9]^T$$

$$= [0.5 + 0.3, \ 0.8 + 0.45]^T = [0.8, \ 1.25]^T$$

$$b_{\text{new}} = 0.1 - (0.05)(-3) = 0.1 + 0.15 = 0.25$$

**Step 5: Verify improvement.**

New prediction: $z_{\text{new}} = (0.8)(2) + (1.25)(3) + 0.25 = 1.6 + 3.75 + 0.25 = 5.6$

New error: $5 - 5.6 = -0.6$, squared error: $(-0.6)^2 = 0.36$.

Old squared error: $(1.5)^2 = 2.25$.

**Loss decreased from 2.25 to 0.36** — gradient descent worked correctly. ✅

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: The Supervised Learning Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Data Collection] --> B[Feature Engineering]
    B --> C[Feature Vector x in R^d]
    C --> D[Hypothesis Class H Selection]
    D --> E[Loss Function L Design]
    E --> F[Optimization via Gradient Descent]
    F --> G[Trained Model f_theta]
    G --> H{Prediction on Test Data}
    H --> I[Evaluation: Loss, Accuracy]
    I --> J{Model Satisfactory?}
    J -- No --> K[Hyperparameter Tuning]
    K --> D
    J -- Yes --> L[Deployment]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- Start at the top with raw data and flow downward through the pipeline.
- The feedback loop (J → K → D) represents **iterative model refinement** — a hallmark of real-world ML development.
- The central node **F (Optimization)** is the computational heart of the entire system.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Loss Function Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ML Task Type] --> B{Regression or Classification?}
    B -- Regression --> C{Outliers Present?}
    C -- No --> D[Use MSE L2 Loss]
    C -- Yes --> E[Use Huber or MAE L1 Loss]
    B -- Classification --> F{Probabilistic Output Needed?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Use Cross-Entropy Log Loss]
    F -- No --> H{Margin-Based Decision?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Use Hinge Loss SVM]
    H -- No --> J[Use Hinge or Cross-Entropy]
    G --> K{Number of Classes?}
    K -- Binary --> L[Binary Cross-Entropy]
    K -- Multi --> M[Categorical Cross-Entropy]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- This decision tree is a **KTU exam favorite** — examiners often ask "Which loss function would you use for problem X?"
- Walk through the tree by answering the diamond questions sequentially.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Gradient Descent Variants Comparison

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Full Batch GD] -->|Uses all N samples| B[Stable, slow, memory-heavy]
    C[Stochastic GD] -->|Uses 1 sample| D[Noisy, fast, low-memory]
    E[Mini-Batch GD] -->|Uses B samples| F[Balanced trade-off]
    A -.->|Compromise| E
    C -.->|Compromise| E
    B --> G[Update per epoch: 1]
    D --> H[Update per epoch: N]
    F --> I[Update per epoch: N divided by B]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- **Full Batch GD** processes the entire dataset before each parameter update — smooth trajectory but slow per update.
- **Stochastic GD (SGD)** uses one example per update — very fast but extremely noisy.
- **Mini-Batch GD** is the practical compromise: batch size $B$ typically 32, 64, 128, or 256.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: Gradient Descent Convergence Visualization (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize theta at random] --> B[Compute Loss L theta]
    B --> C[Compute Gradient nabla L]
    C --> D{L converged less than epsilon?}
    D -- No --> E[Update theta = theta - eta times nabla L]
    E --> F[Increment iteration counter t]
    F --> B
    D -- Yes --> G[Return optimal theta star]
    G --> H[Output: Trained Model]
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- The **termination condition** (D) uses a tolerance $\epsilon$ to decide when to stop.
- The **update step** (E) is the heart of GD — parameters move *downhill* on the loss surface.
- This is the algorithm in its purest form; SGD and mini-batch GD are variants with different data sampling.

---

## 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The Optimization Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph ForwardPass["Forward Pass"]
        X[Input x] --> M[Model f_theta]
        M --> Yhat[Prediction y_hat]
        Y[True Label y] --> Cmp[Compare]
        Yhat --> Cmp
    end
    subgraph BackwardPass["Backward Pass"]
        Cmp --> LF[Compute Loss L]
        LF --> GR[Compute Gradient nabla L]
    end
    subgraph UpdateStep["Update Step"]
        GR --> OPT[Optimizer: theta - eta times nabla L]
        OPT --> M
    end
```

**Diagram Reading Guide:**
- This three-stage loop (**Forward → Backward → Update**) is the universal pattern in ML training, applicable to linear regression, logistic regression, and deep neural networks alike.
- The **optimizer** node is the key decision point — GD, SGD, Adam, RMSprop all fit here.
