Use the mnemonic **"1 for First, 2 for Second"**. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - ☐ **Momentum** adds a *velocity* term to plain gradient descent; it does **not** use a per-parameter adaptive rate. - ☐ **ADAGRAD** uses a *cumulative sum* of squared gradients — its main drawback is that the effective learning rate *monotonically decreases* and can reach zero. - ☐ **RMSProp** fixes ADAGRAD by using an *exponentially weighted moving average* of squared gradients (forgetting the distant past). - ☐ **ADAM = Momentum + RMSProp + Bias Correction** — it tracks both the *mean* ($m_t$) and *variance* ($v_t$) of gradients. - ☐ The **bias-correction** in ADAM is $\hat{m}_t = m_t / (1 - \beta_1^t)$ and $\hat{v}_t = v_t / (1 - \beta_2^t)$ — essential for early iterations. - ☐ **Default ADAM hyperparameter values** to memorize: $\eta = 0.001$, $\beta_1 = 0.9$, $\beta_2 = 0.999$, $\epsilon = 10^{-8}$. - ☐ $\epsilon$ is *always* added inside the square root of the denominator — it prevents division by zero, not the gradient itself. - ☐ In the **ADAM update rule**, $\hat{m}_t$ appears in the **numerator** (scaled by $\eta$) and $\sqrt{\hat{v}_t}$ appears in the **denominator**. - ☐ All four methods still rely on **back propagation** to compute the gradient $\nabla L(W_t)$ — the adaptive mechanisms operate on top of BP, not as a replacement for it. - ☐ For the KTU exam, be prepared to: (a) state the equations, (b) explain *why* each term exists, and (c) solve small numerical updates (typically 2–3 iterations of $W_t$). - ☐ Memorize the **mnemonic** for the four methods in order of complexity: **M**omentum → **A**DAGRAD → **R**MSProp → **A**DAM (**"M-A-R-A"**). - ☐ ADAM is the **default optimizer** in modern deep learning (PyTorch's `torch.optim.Adam`, TensorFlow's `tf.keras.optimizers.Adam`).

topics.title: Decision Trees: Information Gain, Gain Ratio, ID3 Algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBCMT304 - CONCEPTS IN MACHINE LEARNING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Decision Trees: Information Gain, Gain Ratio, ID3 Algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Decision Tree — Formal Definition
A **Decision Tree** is a non-parametric, supervised machine learning algorithm used for both classification and regression tasks. It learns a hierarchy of **if-then-else decision rules** inferred from the features of the training data, producing a tree-structured model where:
- **Internal nodes** represent a test on a feature (attribute).
- **Branches** represent the outcome of that test.
- **Leaf (terminal) nodes** represent a class label (in classification) or a continuous value (in regression).

The path from the **root node** to a leaf represents a series of decisions (rules) that classify an instance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The focus of this module is on the **ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** algorithm proposed by *J. Ross Quinlan (1986)*, which uses a **top-down, greedy, divide-and-conquer** approach to construct the tree. The attribute selection mechanism relies on **Information Theory** concepts — Entropy, Information Gain, and Gain Ratio.

### 1.2 Intuition — The "20 Questions" Analogy
Imagine playing the game of *20 Questions*. You try to identify a hidden object by asking the *most informative* question first (e.g., "Is it alive?" rather than "Is it red?"). Each question narrows the search space significantly.

A Decision Tree behaves exactly like this:
- It picks the **most discriminative attribute first** (the root).
- It then recursively asks the next best question on each subset of data.
- The attribute that *best separates* the classes is selected using **Information Gain** or **Gain Ratio**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world Analogy (Medical Diagnosis):** A doctor asking a patient a sequence of questions — *"Do you have a fever?" → "Is it above 101°F?" → "Any travel history?"* — is essentially building a decision tree. The doctor picks the question that **maximally reduces uncertainty** about the disease.

### 1.3 Key Terminology
- **Entropy (H(S)):** A measure of *impurity* or *disorder* in a dataset.
- **Information Gain (IG):** The reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning the dataset on an attribute.
- **Gain Ratio (GR):** A *normalized* version of Information Gain that penalizes attributes with many distinct values.
- **ID3 Algorithm:** A greedy algorithm that recursively selects the attribute with the highest Information Gain (or Gain Ratio) as the splitting criterion.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need both Information Gain *and* Gain Ratio?** Information Gain is **biased** toward attributes with a large number of distinct values (e.g., `Date`, `ID`). Gain Ratio corrects this bias by introducing a normalization factor called **Split Information** (Intrinsic Value).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Entropy — The Foundation
Entropy, borrowed from **Claude Shannon's Information Theory (1948)**, quantifies the average amount of "surprise" or "uncertainty" inherent in the possible outcomes of a random variable.

For a dataset $S$ containing $c$ classes with proportions $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$:

$$H(S) = - \sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)$$

**Properties of Entropy:**
- $H(S) = 0$ → Dataset is **pure** (all instances belong to one class). No uncertainty.
- $H(S) = 1$ → Dataset is **maximally impure** (binary classes with 50-50 split).
- $0 \le H(S) \le \log_2(c)$ → Bounded by the number of classes.

### 2.2 Conditional Entropy — $H(S \mid A)$
The entropy of dataset $S$ *after* partitioning it on attribute $A$ (with $v$ possible values) is:

$$H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \cdot H(S_v)$$

Where $S_v$ is the subset of $S$ where attribute $A$ has the value $v$. This is a **weighted average** of the entropies of the resulting subsets.

### 2.3 Information Gain — $IG(S, A)$
The reduction in entropy achieved by partitioning $S$ on attribute $A$:

$$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$

The attribute that **maximizes IG** is chosen as the splitting attribute at each node.

### 2.4 Split Information (Intrinsic Value) — $\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)$
This measures the **information generated by splitting** the dataset $S$ into $v$ partitions, corresponding to the $v$ distinct values of attribute $A$:

$$\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = - \sum_{v \in \text{Values}(A)} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2\!\left(\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters:** SplitInfo increases as the number of distinct values of $A$ increases. This penalizes attributes like `Customer_ID` or `Date`, which would otherwise dominate Information Gain.

### 2.5 Gain Ratio — $\text{GR}(S, A)$
The Information Gain normalized by the Split Information:

$$\text{GR}(S, A) = \frac{\text{IG}(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}$$

**C4.5 (the successor to ID3)** uses Gain Ratio. **C5.0** is its modern, commercial successor.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Concept | Formula | Range / Units | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|---------------|-------|
| 1 | **Entropy** | $H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2 p_i$ | $[0, \log_2 c]$ in **bits** | $p_i = 0 \Rightarrow 0 \log 0 = 0$ |
| 2 | **Conditional Entropy** | $H(S \mid A) = \sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} H(S_v)$ | bits | Weighted average of subset entropies |
| 3 | **Information Gain** | $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | bits | Higher IG $\Rightarrow$ Better attribute |
| 4 | **Split Info (Intrinsic Value)** | $\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) = -\sum_{v} \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert} \log_2 \frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$ | bits | Penalizes multi-valued attributes |
| 5 | **Gain Ratio** | $\text{GR}(S, A) = \dfrac{\text{IG}(S, A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S, A)}$ | dimensionless | Used in C4.5; handles IG bias |
| 6 | **Gini Index** *(related)* | $\text{Gini}(S) = 1 - \sum_{i} p_i^2$ | $[0, 1 - \tfrac{1}{c}]$ | Used in CART algorithm |

### 2.7 Real-world Engineering Utility
- **Medical Diagnosis Systems:** Trees for symptom-based disease prediction.
- **Credit Risk Assessment:** Banks use decision trees to decide loan approvals.
- **Fault Diagnosis in Manufacturing:** Identifying root cause of machine failure.
- **Customer Churn Prediction:** Telecom companies predict which customers will leave.
- **Bioinformatics:** Gene expression classification and protein structure prediction.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — The "Play Tennis" Dataset

**Dataset S** (14 instances, 4 attributes, 2 classes: *Yes* / *No*):

| Day | Outlook | Temperature | Humidity | Wind | Play |
|-----|---------|-------------|----------|------|------|
| 1 | Sunny | Hot | High | Weak | No |
| 2 | Sunny | Hot | High | Strong | No |
| 3 | Overcast | Hot | High | Weak | Yes |
| 4 | Rain | Mild | High | Weak | Yes |
| 5 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 6 | Rain | Cool | Normal | Strong | No |
| 7 | Overcast | Cool | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 8 | Sunny | Mild | High | Weak | No |
| 9 | Sunny | Cool | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 10 | Rain | Mild | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 11 | Sunny | Mild | Normal | Strong | Yes |
| 12 | Overcast | Mild | High | Strong | Yes |
| 13 | Overcast | Hot | Normal | Weak | Yes |
| 14 | Rain | Mild | High | Strong | No |

Class distribution: **Yes = 9, No = 5**, total $\vert S \vert = 14$.

---

### 3.2 Step 1 — Compute the Base Entropy $H(S)$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S) &= -\frac{9}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{9}{14}\right) - \frac{5}{14} \log_2\!\left(\frac{5}{14}\right) \\
&= -(0.6429)(-0.6375) - (0.3571)(-1.4854) \\
&= 0.4098 + 0.5305 \\
&= 0.9403 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This is the *initial uncertainty* in the dataset. We want to reduce it.

---

### 3.3 Step 2 — Compute Information Gain for Each Attribute

#### (a) Attribute = **Outlook**
Subsets: *Sunny (5), Overcast (4), Rain (5)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) &= -\tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\tfrac{2}{5} - \tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\tfrac{3}{5} = 0.9710 \\
H(S_{\text{Overcast}}) &= -\tfrac{4}{4}\log_2\tfrac{4}{4} - 0 = 0.0000 \\
H(S_{\text{Rain}}) &= -\tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\tfrac{3}{5} - \tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\tfrac{2}{5} = 0.9710
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S \mid \text{Outlook}) &= \tfrac{5}{14}(0.9710) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0) + \tfrac{5}{14}(0.9710) \\
&= 0.3468 + 0 + 0.3468 = 0.6935
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Outlook}) = 0.9403 - 0.6935 = \mathbf{0.2468} \text{ bits}
$$

#### (b) Attribute = **Temperature**
Subsets: *Hot (4), Mild (6), Cool (4)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Hot}}) &= -\tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\tfrac{2}{4} - \tfrac{2}{4}\log_2\tfrac{2}{4} = 1.0000 \\
H(S_{\text{Mild}}) &= -\tfrac{4}{6}\log_2\tfrac{4}{6} - \tfrac{2}{6}\log_2\tfrac{2}{6} = 0.9183 \\
H(S_{\text{Cool}}) &= -\tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\tfrac{3}{4} - \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\tfrac{1}{4} = 0.8113
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Temp}) = \tfrac{4}{14}(1.0) + \tfrac{6}{14}(0.9183) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0.8113) = 0.9108
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Temperature}) = 0.9403 - 0.9108 = \mathbf{0.0295} \text{ bits}
$$

#### (c) Attribute = **Humidity**
Subsets: *High (7), Normal (7)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{High}}) &= -\tfrac{3}{7}\log_2\tfrac{3}{7} - \tfrac{4}{7}\log_2\tfrac{4}{7} = 0.9852 \\
H(S_{\text{Normal}}) &= -\tfrac{6}{7}\log_2\tfrac{6}{7} - \tfrac{1}{7}\log_2\tfrac{1}{7} = 0.5917
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Humidity}) = \tfrac{7}{14}(0.9852) + \tfrac{7}{14}(0.5917) = 0.7885
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Humidity}) = 0.9403 - 0.7885 = \mathbf{0.1518} \text{ bits}
$$

#### (d) Attribute = **Wind**
Subsets: *Weak (8), Strong (6)*

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(S_{\text{Weak}}) &= -\tfrac{6}{8}\log_2\tfrac{6}{8} - \tfrac{2}{8}\log_2\tfrac{2}{8} = 0.8113 \\
H(S_{\text{Strong}}) &= -\tfrac{3}{6}\log_2\tfrac{3}{6} - \tfrac{3}{6}\log_2\tfrac{3}{6} = 1.0000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Wind}) = \tfrac{8}{14}(0.8113) + \tfrac{6}{14}(1.0) = 0.8922
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Wind}) = 0.9403 - 0.8922 = \mathbf{0.0481} \text{ bits}
$$

---

### 3.4 Step 3 — Analytical Matrix of Results

| Attribute | $H(S \mid A)$ | Information Gain | SplitInfo | Gain Ratio |
|-----------|---------------|------------------|-----------|------------|
| **Outlook** | 0.6935 | **0.2468** ← max | 1.5774 | **0.1564** ← max |
| Temperature | 0.9108 | 0.0295 | 1.5576 | 0.0189 |
| Humidity | 0.7885 | 0.1518 | 1.0000 | 0.1518 |
| Wind | 0.8922 | 0.0481 | 0.9852 | 0.0488 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision:** Since `Outlook` has the **highest Information Gain (0.2468)**, ID3 selects `Outlook` as the **root node**. Note that Gain Ratio also confirms `Outlook` as the best attribute in this example (although this is not always the case — when it isn't, Gain Ratio is preferred as the tie-breaker).

---

### 3.5 Step 4 — Recursive Splitting at the Next Level (Branch = `Sunny`)

Subset for `Sunny` contains 5 instances: **2 Yes, 3 No**. We repeat the process on this subset using the remaining attributes {Temperature, Humidity, Wind}.

$$
H(S_{\text{Sunny}}) = -\tfrac{2}{5}\log_2\tfrac{2}{5} - \tfrac{3}{5}\log_2\tfrac{3}{5} = 0.9710
$$

#### IG for Humidity within Sunny subset:
- Humidity = High: 3 instances (0 Yes, 3 No) → $H = 0$
- Humidity = Normal: 2 instances (2 Yes, 0 No) → $H = 0$

$$
H(S_{\text{Sunny}} \mid \text{Humidity}) = \tfrac{3}{5}(0) + \tfrac{2}{5}(0) = 0
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S_{\text{Sunny}}, \text{Humidity}) = 0.9710 - 0 = \mathbf{0.9710} \text{ (perfect split!)}
$$

#### IG for Wind within Sunny subset:
- Wind = Weak: 3 instances (1 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0.9183$
- Wind = Strong: 2 instances (1 Yes, 1 No) → $H = 1.0$

$$
H(S_{\text{Sunny}} \mid \text{Wind}) = \tfrac{3}{5}(0.9183) + \tfrac{2}{5}(1.0) = 0.9509
$$

$$
\text{IG} = 0.9710 - 0.9509 = 0.0201
$$

#### IG for Temperature within Sunny subset:
- Hot: 2 instances (0 Yes, 2 No) → $H = 0$
- Mild: 2 instances (1 Yes, 1 No) → $H = 1.0$
- Cool: 1 instance (1 Yes, 0 No) → $H = 0$

$$
H(S_{\text{Sunny}} \mid \text{Temp}) = \tfrac{2}{5}(0) + \tfrac{2}{5}(1.0) + \tfrac{1}{5}(0) = 0.4000
$$

$$
\text{IG} = 0.9710 - 0.4000 = 0.5710
$$

**Decision:** Choose **Humidity** for the Sunny branch (highest IG = 0.9710), which gives a **perfectly pure** partition. This recursion continues until all branches terminate in pure leaves or no more attributes are available.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 ID3 Algorithm — Control Flow Diagram

```mermaid
graph TD
    start1[START: Input training set S and attribute list A]
    check1{All instances in S belong to the same class?}
    leaf1[Return leaf node labelled with that class]
    check2{Is attribute list A empty?}
    leaf2[Return leaf labelled with majority class of S]
    calc1[For each attribute a in A: compute IG S a]
    select1[Select attribute a star with maximum IG]
    create1[Create node splitting on a star]
    split1[Partition S into subsets S1 S2 ... Sv for each value v of a star]
    recurse1[For each subset Si: recursively call ID3 on Si and A minus a star]
    attach1[Attach results as children of node]
    return1[Return the constructed decision tree]

    start1 --> check1
    check1 -- YES --> leaf1
    check1 -- NO --> check2
    check2 -- YES --> leaf2
    check2 -- NO --> calc1
    calc1 --> select1
    select1 --> create1
    create1 --> split1
    split1 --> recurse1
    recurse1 --> attach1
    attach1 --> return1
```

### 4.2 Resulting Decision Tree (Play Tennis — Top Two Levels)

```mermaid
graph TD
    root1[OUTLOOK]
    sunny1[SUNNY]
    overcast1[OVERCAST]
    rain1[RAIN]
    leaf1[PLAY = YES]
    nodeHum[HUMIDITY]
    sunny1 --> nodeHum
    nodeHum --> high1[HIGH]
    nodeHum --> normal1[NORMAL]
    high1 --> leaf2[PLAY = NO]
    normal1 --> leaf1
    overcast1 --> leaf1
    rain1 --> nodeWind[WIND]
    nodeWind --> weak1[WEAK]
    nodeWind --> strong1[STRONG]
    weak1 --> leaf1
    strong1 --> leaf2
    root1 --> sunny1
    root1 --> overcast1
    root1 --> rain1
```

### 4.3 Information Gain Computation Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Raw Dataset S] --> B[Compute Class Probabilities p1 ... pc]
    B --> C[Base Entropy H S]
    C --> D[For each candidate attribute A]
    D --> E[Partition S into Subsets S v]
    E --> F[Compute Weighted Conditional Entropy H S given A]
    F --> G[Compute Information Gain IG = H S minus H S given A]
    G --> H{Compare IG values}
    H --> I[Select Attribute with Maximum IG]
    I --> J[Split Node]
    J --> K[Recurse on Subsets]
    K --> A
```

### 4.4 Comparison Block Diagram: ID3 vs C4.5 vs CART

```mermaid
graph TD
    family1[Decision Tree Family]
    id31[ID3]
    c451[C4.5]
    cart1[CART]
    family1 --> id31
    family1 --> c451
    family1 --> cart1
    id31 --> crit1[Splitting Criterion: Information Gain]
    c451 --> crit2[Splitting Criterion: Gain Ratio]
    cart1 --> crit3[Splitting Criterion: Gini Index]
    id31 --> handle1[Handles: Categorical only]
    c451 --> handle2[Handles: Categorical and Continuous with pruning]
    cart1 --> handle3[Handles: Binary splits only]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Define Entropy in the context of Decision Trees. What is its range for a binary classification problem, and what does a value of zero signify?**

**Model Answer:**

**Entropy** is a measure of impurity (or disorder) in a dataset, derived from Claude Shannon's Information Theory. For a dataset $S$ with $c$ classes having probabilities $p_1, p_2, \ldots, p_c$:

$$
H(S) = -\sum_{i=1}^{c} p_i \log_2(p_i)
$$

**Range for binary classification:** $0 \le H(S) \le 1$ (since $\log_2 2 = 1$).

**Significance of $H(S) = 0$:** It signifies that the dataset is **perfectly pure**, meaning all instances belong to a single class. There is no uncertainty or impurity — the decision at that node is finalized.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`
**Differentiate between Information Gain and Gain Ratio. Why is Gain Ratio preferred in some algorithms like C4.5?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Information Gain | Gain Ratio |
|---------|------------------|------------|
| Formula | $\text{IG}(S,A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$ | $\text{GR}(S,A) = \dfrac{\text{IG}(S,A)}{\text{SplitInfo}(S,A)}$ |
| Bias | Biased toward multi-valued attributes | Normalized; unbiased |
| Used in | ID3 | C4.5 |
| Penalty | No penalty for many branches | Penalizes attributes producing many splits |

**Why Gain Ratio is preferred:** Information Gain has a *bias toward attributes with a large number of distinct values* (e.g., `Date`, `Customer_ID`). Such attributes tend to produce many tiny pure subsets, artificially inflating IG. Gain Ratio corrects this by **normalizing** the IG with the Split Information (Intrinsic Value), giving a fair measure of attribute quality. C4.5 thus uses Gain Ratio to overcome ID3's bias.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

**Question A** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** For the dataset given below, compute the **Entropy** of the target attribute `Buy_Computer`. (7 marks)

**(b)** Calculate the **Information Gain** for each feature and identify the attribute that should be selected as the **root node** of the ID3 decision tree. (7 marks)

| Age | Income | Student | Credit_Rating | Buy_Computer |
|-----|--------|---------|---------------|--------------|
| ≤30 | High | No | Fair | No |
| ≤30 | High | No | Excellent | No |
| 31–40 | High | No | Fair | Yes |
| >40 | Medium | No | Fair | Yes |
| >40 | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| >40 | Low | Yes | Excellent | No |
| 31–40 | Low | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| ≤30 | Medium | No | Fair | No |
| ≤30 | Low | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| >40 | Medium | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| ≤30 | Medium | Yes | Excellent | Yes |
| 31–40 | Medium | No | Excellent | Yes |
| 31–40 | High | Yes | Fair | Yes |
| >40 | Medium | No | Excellent | No |

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(a):**

Class distribution: **Yes = 9, No = 5**, total $N = 14$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
H(\text{Buy}) &= -\frac{9}{14}\log_2\!\frac{9}{14} - \frac{5}{14}\log_2\!\frac{5}{14} \\
&= -(0.6429)(-0.6375) - (0.3571)(-1.4854) \\
&= 0.4098 + 0.5305 \\
&= \boxed{0.940 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Q.A(b):**

We compute IG for each attribute. Calculations follow the same procedure as Section 3.3.

**(i) Attribute = Age**
- ≤30 (5): 2 Yes, 3 No → $H = 0.971$
- 31–40 (4): 4 Yes, 0 No → $H = 0$
- >40 (5): 3 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.971$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Age}) = \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0) + \tfrac{5}{14}(0.971) = 0.694
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Age}) = 0.940 - 0.694 = \mathbf{0.246} \text{ bits}
$$

**(ii) Attribute = Income**
- High (4): 2 Yes, 2 No → $H = 1.000$
- Medium (6): 4 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.918$
- Low (4): 3 Yes, 1 No → $H = 0.811$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Income}) = \tfrac{4}{14}(1.0) + \tfrac{6}{14}(0.918) + \tfrac{4}{14}(0.811) = 0.911
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Income}) = 0.940 - 0.911 = \mathbf{0.029} \text{ bits}
$$

**(iii) Attribute = Student**
- No (7): 3 Yes, 4 No → $H = 0.985$
- Yes (7): 6 Yes, 1 No → $H = 0.592$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Student}) = \tfrac{7}{14}(0.985) + \tfrac{7}{14}(0.592) = 0.789
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Student}) = 0.940 - 0.789 = \mathbf{0.152} \text{ bits}
$$

**(iv) Attribute = Credit_Rating**
- Fair (8): 6 Yes, 2 No → $H = 0.811$
- Excellent (6): 3 Yes, 3 No → $H = 1.000$

$$
H(S \mid \text{Credit}) = \tfrac{8}{14}(0.811) + \tfrac{6}{14}(1.0) = 0.892
$$

$$
\text{IG}(S, \text{Credit}) = 0.940 - 0.892 = \mathbf{0.048} \text{ bits}
$$

**Summary Matrix:**

| Attribute | IG |
|-----------|-----|
| **Age** | **0.246** ← max |
| Income | 0.029 |
| Student | 0.152 |
| Credit_Rating | 0.048 |

**Root node decision:** Since `Age` has the **highest Information Gain (0.246 bits)**, the ID3 algorithm will select **`Age`** as the root node for the decision tree.

---

**Question B (Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the **ID3 Algorithm** with its steps. How does it select the splitting attribute at each node? (7 marks)

**(b)** Consider a dataset with 10 training instances where 6 belong to class $C_1$ and 4 to class $C_2$. An attribute $A$ splits this dataset into three subsets as follows: $S_1$ has 4 instances (3 $C_1$, 1 $C_2$), $S_2$ has 3 instances (2 $C_1$, 1 $C_2$), and $S_3$ has 3 instances (1 $C_1$, 2 $C_2$). Compute the **Entropy**, **Conditional Entropy**, **Information Gain**, **Split Information**, and **Gain Ratio** for attribute $A$. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(a):**

**ID3 (Iterative Dichotomiser 3)** is a greedy, top-down, divide-and-conquer algorithm developed by J. Ross Quinlan (1986) to construct a decision tree from categorical data.

**Algorithm Steps:**

1. **Begin at the root node** with the entire training set $S$.
2. **Base Case 1:** If all instances in $S$ belong to the same class, return a single leaf node labeled with that class. **STOP**.
3. **Base Case 2:** If no attributes remain for splitting, return a leaf node labeled with the **majority class** in $S$. **STOP**.
4. **Attribute Selection:** For every remaining attribute $A$, compute its **Information Gain** $\text{IG}(S, A)$ using:
   $$\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$$
5. **Split:** Select the attribute $A^*$ that **maximizes** the Information Gain. Create a decision node splitting on $A^*$.
6. **Recursion:** Partition $S$ into $v$ subsets $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_v$ (one for each value of $A^*$) and **recursively apply ID3** to each subset, removing $A^*$ from the attribute list.
7. **Termination:** The recursion continues until all branches terminate in pure leaf nodes or no more attributes exist.

**Selection Criterion:** ID3 picks the attribute that **maximizes** Information Gain — i.e., the one that produces the **largest reduction in entropy** at each step. This is a **greedy, locally-optimal** choice (not necessarily globally optimal).

---

**Model Solution for Q.B(b):**

**Step 1: Base Entropy $H(S)$**

$$
H(S) = -\tfrac{6}{10}\log_2\tfrac{6}{10} - \tfrac{4}{10}\log_2\tfrac{4}{10} = -(0.6)(-0.737) - (0.4)(-1.322) = 0.4422 + 0.5288 = \boxed{0.9710 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step 2: Entropy of Each Subset**

$$
H(S_1) = -\tfrac{3}{4}\log_2\tfrac{3}{4} - \tfrac{1}{4}\log_2\tfrac{1}{4} = 0.8113
$$

$$
H(S_2) = -\tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\tfrac{2}{3} - \tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\tfrac{1}{3} = 0.9183
$$

$$
H(S_3) = -\tfrac{1}{3}\log_2\tfrac{1}{3} - \tfrac{2}{3}\log_2\tfrac{2}{3} = 0.9183
$$

**Step 3: Conditional Entropy $H(S \mid A)$**

$$
H(S \mid A) = \tfrac{4}{10}(0.8113) + \tfrac{3}{10}(0.9183) + \tfrac{3}{10}(0.9183)
$$
$$
= 0.3245 + 0.2755 + 0.2755 = \boxed{0.8755 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step 4: Information Gain**

$$
\text{IG}(S, A) = 0.9710 - 0.8755 = \boxed{0.0955 \text{ bits}}
$$

**Step 5: Split Information (Intrinsic Value)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SplitInfo}(S, A) &= -\tfrac{4}{10}\log_2\tfrac{4}{10} - \tfrac{3}{10}\log_2\tfrac{3}{10} - \tfrac{3}{10}\log_2\tfrac{3}{10} \\
&= -(0.4)(-1.322) - (0.3)(-1.737) - (0.3)(-1.737) \\
&= 0.5288 + 0.5211 + 0.5211 = \boxed{1.5710 \text{ bits}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6: Gain Ratio**

$$
\text{GR}(S, A) = \frac{0.0955}{1.5710} = \boxed{0.0608 \text{ (dimensionless)}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although the Information Gain is small (0.0955), the Gain Ratio is even smaller because the attribute creates 3 partitions, increasing SplitInfo. Gain Ratio thus penalizes attributes that produce too many splits.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the convention $0 \log_2 0 = 0$** when $p_i = 0$ in entropy sums (deduct 1 mark).
> 2. **Not using $\log_2$** instead of $\ln$ or $\log_{10}$ — the unit MUST be *bits*. (Deduct 1–2 marks).
> 3. **Mixing up Conditional Entropy and Information Gain** — they are *complementary*, not the same. (Common 2-mark error).
> 4. **Forgetting the weight $\frac{\vert S_v \vert}{\vert S \vert}$** in $H(S \mid A)$ — students often compute a *simple average* instead of a *weighted average*. (Deduct 1 mark).
> 5. **Reporting Gain Ratio without first computing SplitInfo** — examiner may give 0 marks for GR part if intermediate steps are missing.
> 6. **Not selecting the best attribute** at the end of an IG calculation — always conclude with a clear "Therefore, attribute X is chosen as the root because it has the highest IG of $Y$ bits."

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

- **Entropy** $H(S)$ measures impurity: $0$ = pure, $\log_2 c$ = maximally impure. Always use **base-2 logarithm**.
- **Information Gain** is the **decrease in entropy** after splitting on an attribute: $\text{IG}(S, A) = H(S) - H(S \mid A)$. Higher = better.
- **Conditional Entropy** $H(S \mid A)$ is a **weighted average** of subset entropies. Do NOT use a simple average.
- **Gain Ratio** normalizes IG by SplitInfo: $\text{GR}(S, A) = \text{IG}(S, A) / \text{SplitInfo}(S, A)$. Used in **C4.5**, not in original **ID3**.
- **SplitInfo** (Intrinsic Value) increases with the number of distinct values — this is the **penalty mechanism** that prevents overfitting to high-cardinality attributes.
- **ID3 Algorithm:** top-down, greedy, recursive, uses Information Gain. Stops when a node is pure or attributes are exhausted.
- **C4.5 = ID3 + Gain Ratio + Handling of continuous attributes + Pruning.**
- **CART** uses **Gini Index** instead of Entropy and produces only **binary trees**.
- **Conventions to memorize:** $0 \log 0 = 0$; entropy range $[0, \log_2 c]$; bits is the standard unit.
- **Bias of IG:** Always check whether the chosen attribute is "fair" — if not, switch to Gain Ratio.
- **Exam Tip:** For every numerical answer, *show the formula, plug in the values, and conclude with a boxed/highlighted final answer*. Always restate which attribute is selected and why.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Clustering?

**Clustering** is an **unsupervised learning** paradigm in machine learning where the objective is to discover natural groupings (clusters) within a dataset $X = \{x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n\}$ such that objects within the same cluster exhibit high **intra-cluster similarity**, while objects in different clusters show high **inter-cluster dissimilarity**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> Given a dataset $X \subset \mathbb{R}^d$ with $n$ unlabeled points, clustering seeks to partition $X$ into $k$ groups $C = \{C_1, C_2, \ldots, C_k\}$ such that the within-cluster sum of squares (WCSS) is minimized:
> $$\text{WCSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$$
> where $\mu_i$ is the centroid (mean) of cluster $C_i$.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "T-Shirt Sorting Room"

Imagine you walk into a massive warehouse containing **10,000 unsorted T-shirts** of different colors, sizes, and brands. There are **no labels**. Your task is to organize them into racks.

- You don't have a supervisor telling you "these are the categories" (that would be *classification*).
- Instead, you group them by **what looks similar** — all reds together, all blues together, all XL together.

That decision-making process — grouping unknown items by inherent similarity — is exactly **clustering**. The "yardstick" you use to decide whether two T-shirts "look similar" is the **similarity measure** (color distance, size distance, fabric texture).

## 1.2 The Three Pillars of Clustering

| Pillar | Purpose |
|---|---|
| **Similarity / Dissimilarity Measure** | Quantifies how "close" or "far" two data points are |
| **Clustering Algorithm** | The strategy used to build the groups (hierarchical, partitional, density-based) |
| **Validation Criterion** | Evaluates the quality of the resulting clusters |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Clustering Matters in Engineering (KTU Context):**
> - **Image segmentation** (medical imaging, satellite imagery)
> - **Anomaly detection** (intrusion detection, fault diagnosis)
> - **Customer segmentation** (recommendation systems, marketing)
> - **Document grouping** (NLP, search engines)
> - **Genomic data analysis** (gene expression clustering)

## 1.3 Similarity vs. Dissimilarity

- **Similarity $s(x_i, x_j)$** — *higher* values indicate *more alike*. Bounded typically in $[-1, 1]$ or $[0, 1]$.
- **Dissimilarity / Distance $d(x_i, x_j)$** — *higher* values indicate *less alike*. Bounded in $[0, \infty)$.

The conversion relation: $s = \frac{1}{1+d}$ or $s = e^{-d^2}$ (Gaussian kernel).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A valid **distance metric** $d(x, y)$ must satisfy four axioms:
> 1. **Non-negativity:** $d(x, y) \geq 0$
> 2. **Identity:** $d(x, y) = 0 \iff x = y$
> 3. **Symmetry:** $d(x, y) = d(y, x)$
> 4. **Triangle inequality:** $d(x, z) \leq d(x, y) + d(y, z)$

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Similarity / Dissimilarity Measures

### 2.1.1 Euclidean Distance ($L_2$ Norm)

The straight-line ("as the crow flies") distance between two points in $d$-dimensional space. The **most commonly used metric** in KTU exam problems.

$$d_{E}(x, y) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d} (x_j - y_j)^2} = \lVert x - y \rVert_2$$

- **Properties:** Scale-sensitive (features with larger ranges dominate). Best for **spherical, isotropic clusters**.

### 2.1.2 Manhattan / City-Block Distance ($L_1$ Norm)

The sum of absolute differences along each axis. Imagine walking on a grid (Manhattan streets).

$$d_{M}(x, y) = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert = \lVert x - y \rVert_1$$

- **Properties:** Less sensitive to outliers than Euclidean. Useful for **high-dimensional sparse data**.

### 2.1.3 Minkowski Distance ($L_p$ Norm) — Generalization

A unified family indexed by parameter $p \geq 1$:

$$d_{p}(x, y) = \left( \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert^{p} \right)^{1/p}$$

- $p = 1 \Rightarrow$ Manhattan
- $p = 2 \Rightarrow$ Euclidean
- $p \to \infty \Rightarrow$ **Chebyshev distance** $\max_j \vert x_j - y_j \vert$

### 2.1.4 Cosine Similarity

Measures the **angle** between two vectors (ignoring magnitude). Widely used in **text mining and document clustering**.

$$s_{C}(x, y) = \frac{x \cdot y}{\lVert x \rVert \cdot \lVert y \rVert} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{d} x_j y_j}{\sqrt{\sum_j x_j^2} \cdot \sqrt{\sum_j y_j^2}}$$

The corresponding **distance** is: $d_{C} = 1 - s_{C}$.

### 2.1.5 Jaccard Similarity (for binary/set data)

For sets $A$ and $B$:
$$J(A, B) = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$$

- $J = 1$ for identical sets, $J = 0$ for disjoint.

## 2.2 Hierarchical Clustering — Theoretical Framework

Hierarchical clustering produces a **tree of clusters** called a **dendrogram**. No need to pre-specify $k$.

### Two Variants

| Approach | Strategy | Complexity |
|---|---|---|
| **Agglomerative** (Bottom-up) | Start with $n$ singleton clusters; repeatedly merge the two closest | $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ optimized |
| **Divisive** (Top-down) | Start with one cluster; recursively split | $O(2^n)$, rarely used |

### 2.2.1 Agglomerative Clustering — Operational Steps

1. Initialize: each point forms its own cluster (total = $n$ clusters).
2. Compute the **pairwise distance matrix** $D \in \mathbb{R}^{n \times n}$.
3. **Repeat** until only 1 cluster remains:
   - Find the pair $(C_i, C_j)$ with the smallest inter-cluster distance.
   - Merge $C_i$ and $C_j$ into a new cluster $C_{ij}$.
   - Update the distance matrix.
4. Cut the dendrogram at the desired level to obtain $k$ clusters.

### 2.2.2 Linkage Methods (How to Measure Inter-Cluster Distance)

Let $C_a$ and $C_b$ be two clusters. The linkage function $\mathcal{L}(C_a, C_b)$ defines the distance between them.

| Linkage | Formula | Tendency |
|---|---|---|
| **Single Linkage** | $\min_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Chaining — produces long, thin clusters |
| **Complete Linkage** | $\max_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Compact, equal-diameter clusters |
| **Average Linkage** | $\frac{1}{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert} \sum_{x \in C_a, y \in C_b} d(x, y)$ | Compromise between single and complete |
| **Ward's Linkage** | $\Delta = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \lVert \mu_a - \mu_b \rVert^2$ | Minimizes within-cluster variance; similar to K-means objective |

## 2.3 Partitional Clustering — Theoretical Framework

In **partitional clustering**, the dataset is directly decomposed into $k$ **non-overlapping** clusters in a single step. There is **no hierarchy**.

### Definition
A $k$-partitional clustering is a function $f: X \to \{1, 2, \ldots, k\}$ assigning each $x_i$ to exactly one cluster.

### Key Distinction from Hierarchical

| Feature | Hierarchical | Partitional |
|---|---|---|
| Output | Dendrogram (tree) | Flat partition |
| Number of clusters | Determined by cut | Must be specified |
| Computational cost | $O(n^2 \log n)$ to $O(n^3)$ | $O(nkdT)$, $T$ = iterations |
| Reversibility | Cannot undo merges | Iterative refinement |

> [!NOTE]
> **K-means is the canonical example of partitional clustering**, while **agglomerative methods are the canonical example of hierarchical clustering.**

## 2.4 K-Means Clustering — The Lloyd Algorithm

**Goal:** Partition $n$ points into $k$ clusters by minimizing the **within-cluster sum of squared errors (SSE)**:

$$J(C) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2, \quad \mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$$

### Algorithm Steps (Lloyd's Iteration)

1. **Initialize:** Choose $k$ initial centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \ldots, \mu_k^{(0)}\}$ randomly.
2. **Assignment step:** For each $x_j$, assign to nearest centroid:
$$C_i^{(t)} = \left\{ x_j : \lVert x_j - \mu_i^{(t)} \rVert^2 \leq \lVert x_j - \mu_l^{(t)} \rVert^2 \ \forall \ l = 1, \ldots, k \right\}$$
3. **Update step:** Recompute centroids as cluster means:
$$\mu_i^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_i^{(t)} \vert} \sum_{x_j \in C_i^{(t)}} x_j$$
4. **Convergence check:** Repeat steps 2–3 until centroids stabilize ($\lVert \mu_i^{(t+1)} - \mu_i^{(t)} \rVert < \epsilon$).

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Euclidean Distance | $d_E = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{d}(x_j - y_j)^2}$ | Default in K-means |
| 2 | Manhattan Distance | $d_M = \sum_{j=1}^{d} \vert x_j - y_j \vert$ | Robust to outliers |
| 3 | Minkowski ($L_p$) | $d_p = \left(\sum \vert x_j - y_j \vert^p\right)^{1/p}$ | $p = 1, 2, \infty$ |
| 4 | Cosine Similarity | $s_C = \frac{x \cdot y}{\lVert x \rVert \lVert y \rVert}$ | Direction-only |
| 5 | Jaccard Index | $J = \frac{\vert A \cap B \vert}{\vert A \cup B \vert}$ | Sets/binary data |
| 6 | K-means Objective | $J = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \lVert x - \mu_i \rVert^2$ | Minimized |
| 7 | Centroid Update | $\mu_i = \frac{1}{\vert C_i \vert} \sum_{x \in C_i} x$ | Mean of cluster |
| 8 | Single Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \min_{x,y} d(x,y)$ | Chaining effect |
| 9 | Complete Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \max_{x,y} d(x,y)$ | Compact clusters |
| 10 | Average Linkage | $d(C_a, C_b) = \text{avg} \ d(x,y)$ | Balanced |
| 11 | Ward's Linkage | $\Delta = \frac{\vert C_a \vert \vert C_b \vert}{\vert C_a \vert + \vert C_b \vert} \lVert \mu_a - \mu_b \rVert^2$ | Variance-minimizing |
| 12 | Time Complexity (K-means) | $O(n \cdot k \cdot T \cdot d)$ | $T$ = iterations |
| 13 | Time Complexity (Agglomerative) | $O(n^3)$ naive, $O(n^2 \log n)$ | Worse for large $n$ |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bioinformatics:** Clustering gene-expression data (Ward linkage) to discover disease subtypes.
- **Computer Vision:** Image compression via K-means on color pixels (vector quantization).
- **Telecommunications:** Customer churn segmentation using cosine similarity on call-detail records.
- **IoT & Sensor Networks:** Anomaly detection in smart-grid readings using Euclidean distance.
- **NLP:** Document clustering using cosine similarity on TF-IDF vectors.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — K-Means Iteration (Step-by-Step, KTU-Style)

**Problem (Frequently asked in KTU University Exam):**
Apply the K-means algorithm with **$k = 2$** on the following 2-D dataset using **Euclidean distance**:
$$X = \{ (2, 10), (2, 5), (8, 4), (5, 8), (7, 5), (6, 4), (1, 2), (4, 9) \}$$
Take initial centroids $\mu_1^{(0)} = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2^{(0)} = (5, 8)$.

### Solution — Iteration 0 → 1

**Step 1: Compute Euclidean distance from each point to both centroids.**

For point $x_1 = (2, 10)$:
$$d(x_1, \mu_1) = \sqrt{(2-2)^2 + (10-10)^2} = 0$$
$$d(x_1, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(2-5)^2 + (10-8)^2} = \sqrt{9+4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$
Assign $x_1 \to C_1$.

For point $x_2 = (2, 5)$:
$$d(x_2, \mu_1) = \sqrt{0 + 25} = 5.00$$
$$d(x_2, \mu_2) = \sqrt{9 + 9} = \sqrt{18} \approx 4.24$$
Assign $x_2 \to C_2$.

For point $x_3 = (8, 4)$:
$$d(x_3, \mu_1) = \sqrt{36 + 36} = \sqrt{72} \approx 8.49$$
$$d(x_3, \mu_2) = \sqrt{9 + 16} = \sqrt{25} = 5.00$$
Assign $x_3 \to C_2$.

For point $x_4 = (5, 8)$:
$$d(x_4, \mu_1) = \sqrt{9 + 4} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$
$$d(x_4, \mu_2) = \sqrt{0 + 0} = 0$$
Assign $x_4 \to C_2$.

For point $x_5 = (7, 5)$:
$$d(x_5, \mu_1) = \sqrt{25 + 25} = \sqrt{50} \approx 7.07$$
$$d(x_5, \mu_2) = \sqrt{4 + 9} = \sqrt{13} \approx 3.61$$
Assign $x_5 \to C_2$.

For point $x_6 = (6, 4)$:
$$d(x_6, \mu_1) = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$$
$$d(x_6, \mu_2) = \sqrt{1 + 16} = \sqrt{17} \approx 4.12$$
Assign $x_6 \to C_2$.

For point $x_7 = (1, 2)$:
$$d(x_7, \mu_1) = \sqrt{1 + 64} = \sqrt{65} \approx 8.06$$
$$d(x_7, \mu_2) = \sqrt{16 + 36} = \sqrt{52} \approx 7.21$$
Assign $x_7 \to C_2$.

For point $x_8 = (4, 9)$:
$$d(x_8, \mu_1) = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$
$$d(x_8, \mu_2) = \sqrt{1 + 1} = \sqrt{2} \approx 1.41$$
Assign $x_8 \to C_2$.

**Summary after Assignment Step:**
- $C_1 = \{(2, 10)\}$
- $C_2 = \{(2, 5), (8, 4), (5, 8), (7, 5), (6, 4), (1, 2), (4, 9)\}$

**Step 2: Update Centroids.**

For $C_1$:
$$\mu_1^{(1)} = (2, 10)$$

For $C_2$:
$$\mu_2^{(1)} = \left( \frac{2+8+5+7+6+1+4}{7}, \ \frac{5+4+8+5+4+2+9}{7} \right) = \left( \frac{33}{7}, \ \frac{37}{7} \right) \approx (4.71, 5.29)$$

### Iteration 1 → 2

**Re-assign all points using new centroids $\mu_1 = (2, 10)$ and $\mu_2 \approx (4.71, 5.29)$.**

For $x_8 = (4, 9)$:
$$d(x_8, \mu_1) = \sqrt{4 + 1} = \sqrt{5} \approx 2.24$$
$$d(x_8, \mu_2) = \sqrt{(4-4.71)^2 + (9-5.29)^2} = \sqrt{0.50 + 13.76} \approx 3.78$$
Assign $x_8 \to C_1$.

After re-evaluation (similar calculations omitted for brevity, but follow the identical procedure):
- $C_1 = \{(2, 10), (4, 9)\}$
- $C_2 = \{(2, 5), (8, 4), (5, 8), (7, 5), (6, 4), (1, 2)\}$

**Update centroids:**
$$\mu_1^{(2)} = \left( \frac{2+4}{2}, \ \frac{10+9}{2} \right) = (3.0, 9.5)$$

$$\mu_2^{(2)} = \left( \frac{2+8+5+7+6+1}{6}, \ \frac{5+4+8+5+4+2}{6} \right) = \left( \frac{29}{6}, \ \frac{28}{6} \right) \approx (4.83, 4.67)$$

### Iteration 2 → 3 — Convergence Check

Re-assignment of all 8 points shows **no change** in cluster membership. Centroids remain stable. **Algorithm has converged.**

**Final clusters:**
- $C_1 = \{(2, 10), (4, 9)\}$, centroid $(3.0, 9.5)$
- $C_2 = \{(2, 5), (8, 4), (5, 8), (7, 5), (6, 4), (1, 2)\}$, centroid $(4.83, 4.67)$

**Final SSE (objective value):**
$$J = (2-3)^2 + (10-9.5)^2 + (2-4.83)^2 + (5-4.67)^2 + \ldots = \text{(sum all 8 squared errors)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key observation for KTU exams:** The objective $J$ is **non-increasing** across iterations. K-means always converges to a **local minimum** (not necessarily global).

## 3.2 Worked Example — Agglomerative Clustering with Single Linkage

**Problem:** Cluster the points $A=(1,1), B=(2,1), C=(5,4), D=(6,5)$ using **single linkage** and Euclidean distance.

### Solution

**Step 1: Compute initial distance matrix $D^{(0)}$.**

$$D^{(0)} = \begin{bmatrix}
 & A & B & C & D \\
A & 0 & 1 & 5 & 7.07 \\
B & 1 & 0 & 4.24 & 6.40 \\
C & 5 & 4.24 & 0 & 1.41 \\
D & 7.07 & 6.40 & 1.41 & 0
\end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2: First merge.** Minimum distance is $d(A, B) = 1$. Merge into cluster $(A, B)$.

**Step 3: Recompute distances using single linkage rule:**

$$d((A,B), C) = \min\{d(A,C), d(B,C)\} = \min(5, 4.24) = 4.24$$

$$d((A,B), D) = \min\{d(A,D), d(B,D)\} = \min(7.07, 6.40) = 6.40$$

$$d(C, D) = 1.41$$

**Step 4: Second merge.** Minimum is $d(C, D) = 1.41$. Merge into $(C, D)$.

**Step 5: Final merge.** Merge $(A, B)$ and $(C, D)$ with distance:
$$d((A,B), (C,D)) = \min\{d(A,C), d(A,D), d(B,C), d(B,D)\} = \min(5, 7.07, 4.24, 6.40) = 4.24$$

**Dendrogram structure:** $((((A,B), (C,D))))$ with merge heights $1, 1.41, 4.24$.

## 3.3 Comparison Matrix — When to Use What

| Scenario | Recommended Approach | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Large dataset ($n > 10{,}000$) | K-means | Agglomerative is $O(n^2)$ memory |
| Unknown $k$ | Agglomerative + dendrogram cut | Visual decision |
| Non-spherical clusters | Single-link agglomerative | Detects elongated shapes |
| Compact, equal-size clusters | K-means or Ward linkage | Minimizes WCSS |
| Text / high-dim sparse data | K-means + cosine distance | Handles sparsity |
| Noise robustness needed | K-medoids (not in syllabus but relevant) | Uses actual points as centers |
| Streaming data | Mini-batch K-means | Online updates |

## 3.4 Derivation of K-means Convergence

> [!NOTE]
> **Proof of Convergence (board-relevant sketch):**
>
> K-means is guaranteed to converge in finite steps because:
>
> 1. There are **at most $k^n$** distinct partitions of $n$ points into $k$ clusters.
> 2. The objective $J$ is **bounded below** by $0$.
> 3. Each iteration **strictly decreases or maintains** $J$:
>    - Assignment step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. cluster assignments (given fixed centroids).
>    - Update step minimizes $J$ w.r.t. centroids (given fixed assignments, the mean is the unique minimizer of the sum of squared distances).
> 4. Since $J$ is monotonically non-increasing and the state space is finite, convergence is guaranteed in $O(k^n)$ iterations (practically, in tens to hundreds).
>
> **Limitation:** Convergence is to a **local minimum**, not global. The result is sensitive to initialization.

## 3.5 Initialization Strategies

| Method | Description |
|---|---|
| **Random** | Pick $k$ random points as initial centroids |
| **Forgy** | Pick $k$ points uniformly at random from $X$ |
| **K-means++** | Probabilistic seeding — first centroid random, each subsequent chosen with probability $\propto D(x)^2$ (distance squared to nearest existing centroid) |
| **Random Partition** | Assign each point to a random cluster, then compute initial means |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **K-means++ (Arthur & Vassilvitskii, 2007)** is the default in scikit-learn and provides $O(\log k)$-competitive approximation to the optimal SSE.
