# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Project 1: MNIST Handwritten Digit Classification — Complete Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.1.1 Dataset Characterization

The **MNIST dataset** (Modified National Institute of Standards and Technology) is the de facto benchmark for image classification.

$$\text{MNIST} = \{(x_i, y_i)\}_{i=1}^{N}, \quad x_i \in \mathbb{R}^{28 \times 28}, \quad y_i \in \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 9\}$$

- **Training set size**: $N_{\text{train}} = 60{,}000$
- **Test set size**: $N_{\text{test}} = 10{,}000$
- **Image dimensions**: $28 \times 28$ grayscale pixels → **784-dimensional feature vector** after flattening
- **Pixel intensity range**: $[0, 255]$ (unsigned 8-bit)

> [!NOTE]
> **Flattening operation:** Each $28 \times 28$ matrix is reshaped into a column vector:
> $$x_i = \text{vec}(I_i) \in \mathbb{R}^{784}$$
> where $I_i$ is the $i$-th image matrix. This transformation is necessary because classical ML algorithms (k-NN, SVM, Logistic Regression) expect 1D input vectors.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Methodology

**Step 1: Data Loading and Initial Inspection**

$$X \in \mathbb{R}^{60000 \times 784}, \quad y \in \{0,\ldots,9\}^{60000}$$

Verify class distribution:
$$P(y = k) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \mathbb{1}[y_i = k], \quad \forall k \in \{0,\ldots,9\}$$

For MNIST, $P(y=k) \approx 0.10$ for all $k$ (approximately balanced).

**Step 2: Pre-processing**

Apply **min-max normalization** to scale pixel values to $[0, 1]$:

$$x_i^{\text{norm}} = \frac{x_i - x_{\min}}{x_{\max} - x_{\min}} = \frac{x_i}{255}$$

**Justification**: Normalization ensures that no single pixel dominates the distance metric in k-NN or the regularization term in logistic regression. Without normalization, a pixel with range $[0, 255]$ would contribute $\sim 255 \times$ more to a Euclidean distance than a binary feature.

**Step 3: Dimensionality Reduction (Optional but Recommended)**

Apply **Principal Component Analysis (PCA)** to retain 95% of variance:

$$\text{Find } W \in \mathbb{R}^{784 \times d} \text{ such that } \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{d} \lambda_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{784} \lambda_j} \geq 0.95$$

where $\lambda_j$ are the eigenvalues of the covariance matrix $\Sigma = \frac{1}{N}X^T X$.

Typically, $d \approx 154$ for MNIST at 95% variance retention — a **5× compression** with minimal information loss.

**Step 4: Model Training — Three-Class Comparison**

**Model A: k-Nearest Neighbors (k-NN)**

$$\hat{y}(x_{\text{new}}) = \text{mode}\{y_i : x_i \in N_k(x_{\text{new}})\}$$

where $N_k(x_{\text{new}})$ is the set of $k$ training points closest to $x_{\text{new}}$ under Euclidean distance:

$$d(x_a, x_b) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{784} (x_{a,j} - x_{b,j})^2}$$

**Model B: Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF Kernel**

$$\hat{y}(x) = \text{sign}\left(\sum_{i=1}^{N} \alpha_i y_i K(x_i, x) + b\right)$$

where the RBF kernel is:
$$K(x_i, x_j) = \exp\left(-\gamma \vert\vert x_i - x_j \vert\vert^2\right)$$

**Model C: Random Forest (Bagging Ensemble)**

$$\hat{f}_{\text{RF}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} T_b(x)$$

where each $T_b$ is a decision tree trained on a bootstrap sample with random feature subsets at each split.

**Step 5: Ensemble — Voting Classifier**

$$\hat{y}_{\text{ensemble}}(x) = \arg\max_{k \in \{0,\ldots,9\}} \sum_{j=1}^{M} w_j \cdot \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_j(x) = k]$$

For **soft voting** (using predicted probabilities):

$$\hat{y}_{\text{soft}}(x) = \arg\max_{k} \sum_{j=1}^{M} w_j \cdot P_j(y = k \mid x)$$

**Step 6: Evaluation**

Compute the full metric suite on the held-out test set:

$$\text{Confusion Matrix } C \in \mathbb{R}^{10 \times 10}, \quad C_{ij} = \sum_{n=1}^{N_{\text{test}}} \mathbb{1}[\hat{y}_n = i \land y_n = j]$$

$$\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{10} C_{ii}}{\sum_{i=1}^{10}\sum_{j=1}^{10} C_{ij}}$$

### 3.1.3 Expected Performance Benchmark (KTU Reference Values)

| Algorithm | Expected Test Accuracy | Computation Time |
|---|---|---|
| k-NN (k=3, Euclidean) | ~96.5% | High (lazy learner) |
| Logistic Regression | ~92.5% | Low |
| SVM (RBF kernel) | ~98.0% | Very High |
| Random Forest (100 trees) | ~97.0% | Medium |
| **Voting Ensemble (k-NN + SVM + RF)** | **~98.5%** | High |
| Simple ANN (MLP) | ~98.0% | Medium |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The **ensemble always outperforms the best individual model** — this is the central pedagogical point of Module 4. The improvement (typically 0.3%–0.8%) may seem small in absolute terms, but in competitions like Kaggle, this margin separates the top 1% from the top 10%.

## 3.2 Project 2: Student Examination Performance Prediction — Complete Analytical Walkthrough

### 3.2.1 Dataset Construction Methodology

**Features to Collect** (from institutional records):

| Feature Symbol | Description | Data Type | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| $x_1$ | Internal Test 1 marks | Continuous | $[0, 50]$ |
| $x_2$ | Internal Test 2 marks | Continuous | $[0, 50]$ |
| $x_3$ | Internal Test 3 marks | Continuous | $[0, 50]$ |
| $x_4$ | Assignment average | Continuous | $[0, 10]$ |
| $x_5$ | Attendance percentage | Continuous | $[0, 100]$ |
| $x_6$ | Lab record marks | Continuous | $[0, 20]$ |
| $x_7$ | Seminar/presentation score | Continuous | $[0, 10]$ |
| $y$ (target) | Final University Exam Score | Continuous | $[0, 100]$ |

**Sample size recommendation**: $N \geq 200$ for reliable model training (ideally $N \geq 500$ across multiple batches).

### 3.2.2 Step-by-Step Methodology

**Step 1: Exploratory Data Analysis**

Compute the **Pearson correlation matrix**:

$$r_{jk} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{i,j} - \bar{x}_j)(x_{i,k} - \bar{x}_k)}{\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{i,j} - \bar{x}_j)^2 \cdot \sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{i,k} - \bar{x}_k)^2}}$$

**Expected finding**: $r(x_1, y) \approx 0.65$, $r(x_2, y) \approx 0.68$, $r(x_5, y) \approx 0.45$ — internal test marks and attendance are the strongest predictors.

**Step 2: Feature Engineering**

Create derived features:

$$x_8 = \text{Internal Average} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + x_3}{3}$$

$$x_9 = \text{Consistency} = 1 - \frac{\sigma(x_1, x_2, x_3)}{\mu(x_1, x_2, x_3)}$$

$$x_{10} = \text{Risk Flag} = \mathbb{1}[x_5 < 75] \quad \text{(binary: 1 = at-risk, 0 = safe)}$$

**Step 3: Regression Models — Three-Class Comparison**

**Model A: Multiple Linear Regression**

$$\hat{y} = \beta_0 + \sum_{j=1}^{p} \beta_j x_j + \epsilon$$

**Least Squares Estimate** (closed-form solution):

$$\boldsymbol{\hat{\beta}} = (X^T X)^{-1} X^T \mathbf{y}$$

**Model B: Random Forest Regressor**

$$\hat{f}_{\text{RF}}(x) = \frac{1}{B}\sum_{b=1}^{B} T_b(x)$$

where each tree minimizes the **Mean Squared Error** at each node:

$$\text{MSE}_{\text{node}} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{node}}}\sum_{i \in \text{node}} (y_i - \bar{y}_{\text{node}})^2$$

**Model C: Gradient Boosting Regressor**

$$\hat{f}_M(x) = \hat{f}_0(x) + \sum_{m=1}^{M} \nu \cdot h_m(x)$$

where each $h_m$ is a shallow tree (depth 3–5) fit to the **residuals** of the previous ensemble:

$$r_i^{(m)} = y_i - \hat{f}_{m-1}(x_i)$$

and $\nu \in (0, 1]$ is the **learning rate** (shrinkage factor).

**Step 4: Stacking Ensemble**

**Layer 1 (Base Learners)**: Linear Regression, Random Forest, Gradient Boosting
**Layer 2 (Meta-Learner)**: Ridge Regression

$$\hat{y}_{\text{stack}} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \hat{f}_{\text{LR}}(x) + \beta_2 \hat{f}_{\text{RF}}(x) + \beta_3 \hat{f}_{\text{GB}}(x)$$

The meta-learner learns **optimal weights** for combining base predictions, rather than using simple averaging.

**Step 5: Clustering for At-Risk Student Identification**

Apply **k-Means clustering** with $k = 3$:

1. Initialize $k$ centroids $\{\mu_1^{(0)}, \mu_2^{(0)}, \mu_3^{(0)}\}$ using **k-means++** initialization.
2. **Assignment step**:
$$C_i^{(t)} = \arg\min_{k \in \{1,2,3\}} \vert\vert x_i - \mu_k^{(t)} \vert\vert^2$$
3. **Update step**:
$$\mu_k^{(t+1)} = \frac{1}{\vert C_k \vert}\sum_{i \in C_k} x_i$$
4. Iterate until convergence: $\vert\vert \mu_k^{(t+1)} - \mu_k^{(t)} \vert\vert < \epsilon$.

**Interpretation of Clusters**:
- **Cluster 1 (High Performers)**: $\bar{y} \approx 85$, $\bar{x}_5 \approx 92\%$
- **Cluster 2 (Average Performers)**: $\bar{y} \approx 65$, $\bar{x}_5 \approx 80\%$
- **Cluster 3 (At-Risk)**: $\bar{y} \approx 40$, $\bar{x}_5 \approx 55\%$

**Step 6: Evaluation**

For regression models, compute on the test set:

$$\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N_{\text{test}}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{test}}} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$$

$$\text{MAE} = \frac{1}{N_{\text{test}}}\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{test}}} \vert y_i - \hat{y}_i \vert$$

$$R^2 = 1 - \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{test}}} (y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{N_{\text{test}}} (y_i - \bar{y}_{\text{test}})^2}$$

For clustering, compute:

$$s = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

where $a(i)$ = mean intra-cluster distance and $b(i)$ = mean nearest-cluster distance.

### 3.2.3 Expected Performance Benchmark (KTU Reference Values)

| Algorithm | Expected RMSE | Expected R² |
|---|---|---|
| Linear Regression (baseline) | ~8.5 | ~0.72 |
| Random Forest (100 trees) | ~6.8 | ~0.83 |
| Gradient Boosting | ~6.2 | ~0.86 |
| **Stacking Ensemble** | **~5.9** | **~0.88** |
| k-Means Silhouette Score | — | ~0.52 |

## 3.3 Comparative Analytical Matrix: The Two Projects Side-by-Side

| Dimension | Project 1: MNIST | Project 2: Student Performance |
|---|---|---|
| **Learning Paradigm** | Supervised (Classification) | Supervised (Regression) + Unsupervised (Clustering) |
| **Data Modality** | Image (28×28 grayscale) | Tabular (mixed numerical features) |
| **Dataset Source** | Public benchmark (MNIST) | Self-collected institutional data |
| **Sample Size** | 70,000 (very large) | 200–500 (small to medium) |
| **Curse of Dimensionality Risk** | High (784 features) → PCA recommended | Low (7–10 features) |
| **Class/Output Balance** | Balanced (10 classes) | Continuous output — skewness check needed |
| **Primary Evaluation** | Accuracy, F1, Confusion Matrix | RMSE, R², MAE |
| **Ensemble Strategy** | Hard/Soft Voting | Stacking with Ridge meta-learner |
| **Unsupervised Component** | Optional (t-SNE visualization) | Mandatory (k-Means for risk segmentation) |
| **Ethical Considerations** | MNIT data is anonymized and public | Student data requires IRB/institutional approval |
| **Modules Tested** | Modules 1, 2, 4 (Classification + Ensembles) | Modules 1, 2, 3, 4 (All paradigms) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Hidden Criterion:** The second project is **deliberately harder** because it requires students to demonstrate competence across **all four modules** of the syllabus. Students who choose Project 2 and execute it well typically receive higher project marks because the examiner sees evidence of broader learning.

## 4.5 Cross-Validation Workflow (k-Fold)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Full Dataset] --> B[Shuffle Randomly]
    B --> C[Partition into k equal folds]
    C --> D1[Fold 1 as Validation]
    C --> D2[Fold 2 as Validation]
    C --> D3[Fold 3 as Validation]
    C --> D4[Continuing to Fold k as Validation]
    D1 --> E1[Train on k minus 1 folds, Validate on Fold 1]
    D2 --> E2[Train on k minus 1 folds, Validate on Fold 2]
    D3 --> E3[Train on k minus 1 folds, Validate on Fold 3]
    D4 --> E4[Train on k minus 1 folds, Validate on Fold k]
    E1 --> F[Collect k scores]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[Compute Mean and Standard Deviation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Guide:**
> - All diagrams use the **Kerala University standard left-to-right / top-to-bottom flow** convention.
> - Subgraphs (`subgraph`) are used to visually group related stages, mimicking the section structure of a KTU project report.
> - Node IDs (e.g., `M1`, `S4`) are alphanumeric to comply with Mermaid v10+ parser requirements.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q: Define a machine learning project as per the KTU 2024 syllabus framework. List the six universal stages of any ML project pipeline.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Machine Learning Project** under the KTU 2024 scheme is a structured engineering investigation that solves a real-world problem by learning patterns from data, rather than by explicitly programmed rules.

The **six universal stages** are:
1. **Problem Formalization** — Converting a domain problem into a mathematical learning task (classification/regression/clustering).
2. **Data Acquisition** — Collecting a standard dataset (e.g., MNIST) or building a custom dataset (e.g., institutional records).
3. **Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA)** — Statistical summaries and visualizations to understand data distribution and relationships.
4. **Pre-processing** — Handling missing values, normalizing features, encoding categorical variables, and reducing dimensionality.
5. **Model Building & Training** — Splitting data, training multiple algorithms, tuning hyperparameters via cross-validation.
6. **Evaluation & Interpretation** — Quantifying performance using problem-appropriate metrics (accuracy, F1, RMSE, R², Silhouette score).

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** 1 mark for the definition + 2 marks for the six stages. Students who list only 4–5 stages lose 0.5–1 mark. Write all six explicitly.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]

**Q: Differentiate between the evaluation metrics used in MNIST digit classification (Project 1) and Student Performance Prediction (Project 2). Why is accuracy alone insufficient for both?**

**Model Answer:**

| Dimension | Project 1: MNIST | Project 2: Student Performance |
|---|---|---|
| **Problem Type** | Multi-class Classification | Regression |
| **Primary Metrics** | Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1, Confusion Matrix, ROC-AUC | RMSE, MAE, R², Adjusted R² |
| **Secondary Metrics** | Per-class accuracy, Macro/Weighted F1 | Mean Absolute Percentage Error (MAPE) |
| **Why Accuracy Alone Fails** | In imbalanced variants (e.g., MNIST-M, medical images), a model predicting only the majority class can achieve 90% accuracy while being useless | Accuracy is undefined for continuous outputs; even if discretized, it ignores the magnitude of prediction errors |

**Key Insight**: Evaluation metrics must be **aligned with the problem type and business cost**. Accuracy is a classification metric; regression requires error-based metrics; clustering requires internal validity indices.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip (3 marks):** 1 mark for identifying the metric categories + 1 mark for correct metric names per project + 1 mark for explaining why accuracy alone is insufficient.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks with **internal choice** — students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. Both questions carry equal marks. Each question has two sub-parts: (a) carries 7 marks and (b) carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks): Project 1 — MNIST Handwritten Digit Classification

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**"Design a complete machine learning pipeline to classify handwritten digits (0–9) from the MNIST dataset. Your design must include pre-processing steps, at least three classification algorithms, and a justification for the choice of evaluation metrics. Explain how ensemble methods can improve the classification accuracy."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Problem Definition [1 mark]**

The task is a **10-class single-label multi-class classification** problem:
$$f: \mathbb{R}^{784} \rightarrow \{0, 1, 2, \ldots, 9\}$$

**Step 2: Pre-processing Pipeline [2 marks]**

- **Flattening**: Convert each $28 \times 28$ image into a 784-dimensional vector.
- **Normalization**: Apply min-max scaling: $x' = x / 255$ to bring all features to $[0, 1]$.
- **PCA Dimensionality Reduction**: Retain 95% variance, reducing from 784 to ~154 dimensions, which:
  - Reduces computational cost by ~5×
  - Removes correlated pixel noise
  - Mitigates the curse of dimensionality for distance-based algorithms

**Step 3: Three Classification Algorithms [2 marks]**

| Algorithm | Justification |
|---|---|
| **k-NN (k=3)** | Simple baseline; non-parametric; works well on well-clustered data like MNIST |
| **SVM (RBF kernel)** | Handles high-dimensional data; finds optimal decision boundary; strong on MNIST (~98%) |
| **Random Forest (100 trees)** | Ensemble by itself (bagging); provides feature importance; robust to overfitting |

**Step 4: Ensemble Method [1 mark]**

Construct a **Soft Voting Classifier**:
$$\hat{y}(x) = \arg\max_{k \in \{0,\ldots,9\}} \sum_{j=1}^{3} w_j \cdot P_j(y = k \mid x)$$

**Why this works**: Different algorithms make **uncorrelated errors**. k-NN fails on ambiguous digits (e.g., 4 vs 9), SVM fails on distorted strokes, Random Forest fails on rare patterns. Voting averages out these complementary errors.

**Step 5: Evaluation Metrics [1 mark]**

- **Confusion Matrix** (10×10) to identify which digit pairs are most confused.
- **Per-class Precision, Recall, F1** to detect class-specific weaknesses.
- **Macro F1** to ensure no class is neglected despite balanced data.
- **ROC-AUC (One-vs-Rest)** to visualize the trade-off between sensitivity and specificity for each digit.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**"During evaluation of the MNIST classifier, you observe that the model confuses digit '4' with digit '9' in 8% of test cases. Explain the root cause, propose TWO strategies to mitigate this confusion, and discuss how you would validate that the strategies are effective. Include relevant formulas."**

**Model Solution:**

**Root Cause Analysis [2 marks]**

The visual similarity between handwritten '4' and '9' is the primary cause. In the feature space, the Euclidean distance between the centroid of class 4 and class 9 is small:
$$d(\mu_4, \mu_9) = \sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{784} (\mu_{4,j} - \mu_{9,j})^2} \ll d(\mu_4, \mu_0)$$

The **Bayes error rate** for this pair is non-zero because some handwritten samples are inherently ambiguous even for humans.

**Strategy 1: Data Augmentation [2 marks]**

Apply **affine transformations** to artificially expand the training set:
$$x_{\text{aug}} = A \cdot x + t$$

where $A$ is a rotation/scaling matrix and $t$ is a translation vector. For example:
- Random rotation: $\theta \in [-15°, +15°]$
- Random translation: $\Delta x, \Delta y \in [-2, +2]$ pixels
- Random zoom: scale $\in [0.9, 1.1]$

This forces the model to learn **rotation-invariant and translation-invariant features**, reducing confusion between similarly-shaped digits.

**Strategy 2: Class-Weighted Loss Function [2 marks]**

Modify the SVM or Random Forest to penalize misclassifications of class 4 and 9 more heavily:

$$L_{\text{weighted}} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} w_{y_i} \cdot L(f(x_i), y_i)$$

where the class weight is set as:
$$w_k = \frac{N}{K \cdot N_k}$$

with $N_k$ being the number of samples in class $k$. This forces the decision boundary to **shift away from the ambiguous region** between classes 4 and 9.

**Validation [1 mark]**

- Re-train the model with both strategies and compute the **new confusion matrix** $C'$.
- Verify that $C'_{4,9}$ and $C'_{9,4}$ have decreased.
- Conduct a **paired McNemar's test** to confirm the improvement is statistically significant at $\alpha = 0.05$:
$$\chi^2 = \frac{(b - c)^2}{b + c}$$
where $b$ = number of samples misclassified by Model 1 but not Model 2, and $c$ = vice versa.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students write "use deep learning/CNN" to solve the confusion. While true, this is **out of syllabus** for PBCMT304 (which focuses on classical ML). The examiner will **deduct 2 marks** for suggesting out-of-scope solutions.
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Students forget to justify the choice of augmentation parameters. Writing "I rotated images by 45°" is wrong — such heavy rotation destroys digit structure. Always justify the range.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Students confuse **precision** with **recall** when describing class 4 vs 9 performance. The examiner checks definitions rigorously.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Forgetting to mention **statistical significance testing**. A 1% improvement may be due to random chance. The McNemar's test formula is expected.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Project 2 — Student Performance Prediction

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Q: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**"You are tasked with building a machine learning system to predict final university examination scores of B.Tech students from their internal assessment marks, attendance, and assignment scores. Describe the complete methodology including data collection, feature engineering, model selection, and evaluation. Justify why a stacking ensemble is preferred over simple averaging for this regression task."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Data Collection Strategy [1 mark]**

Collect historical data from the institution's academic records over the past 3–5 years. For each student, record:
- Three internal test scores (out of 50 each)
- Assignment average (out of 10)
- Attendance percentage (0–100%)
- Lab record marks (out of 20)
- Final university exam score (target, out of 100)

Recommended sample size: $N \geq 200$ students; ideally $N \geq 500$ for robust generalization.

**Step 2: Feature Engineering [2 marks]**

Create derived features that capture latent patterns:

$$x_{\text{avg}} = \frac{x_1 + x_2 + x_3}{3} \quad \text{(Internal Average)}$$

$$x_{\text{consistency}} = 1 - \frac{\sigma(x_1, x_2, x_3)}{\mu(x_1, x_2, x_3)} \quad \text{(Performance Consistency)}$$

$$x_{\text{improvement}} = x_3 - x_1 \quad \text{(Trend over semester)}$$

$$x_{\text{risk}} = \mathbb{1}[x_{\text{attendance}} < 75\%] \quad \text{(Binary risk flag)}$$

**Justification**: Raw internal marks alone may not capture the **trajectory** of student performance. A student improving from 20 → 40 is qualitatively different from one declining from 45 → 25, even if the average is similar.

**Step 3: Model Selection [2 marks]**

Choose three diverse base learners:

| Model | Strength |
|---|---|
| **Linear Regression** | Interpretable; provides baseline; reveals linear trends |
| **Random Forest Regressor** | Captures non-linear interactions; robust to outliers |
| **Gradient Boosting (XGBoost)** | Sequentially corrects errors; high accuracy on tabular data |

**Step 4: Stacking Ensemble Construction [2 marks]**

**Layer 0 (Base Learners)**: Generate out-of-fold predictions for the meta-learner:
$$\hat{f}_{-j}^{(-k)}(x_i) = \text{Train on folds} \neq k, \text{Predict on fold } k$$

**Layer 1 (Meta-Learner)**: A **Ridge Regression** that learns optimal weights:
$$\hat{y}_{\text{stack}} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \hat{f}_{\text{LR}}(x) + \beta_2 \hat{f}_{\text{RF}}(x) + \beta_3 \hat{f}_{\text{GB}}(x) + \lambda \sum_{j=1}^{3} \beta_j^2$$

**Why Stacking > Simple Averaging**:
- Simple averaging assigns equal weights: $\hat{y} = \frac{1}{3}(\hat{y}_{\text{LR}} + \hat{y}_{\text{RF}} + \hat{y}_{\text{GB}})$, which is **suboptimal** when models have different accuracies.
- Stacking **learns** the optimal weights from data. If Gradient Boosting has lower RMSE than Linear Regression, the meta-learner will assign a higher $\beta_3$ automatically.
- Stacking can capture **complementary errors** that simple averaging cannot.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**Q: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model]**

**"After building the regression model, the institution wants to identify 'at-risk' students early in the semester. Design a clustering-based approach using k-Means to segment students into risk categories. Explain how the Elbow Method and Silhouette Analysis are used to choose the optimal number of clusters. Describe the evaluation metrics for clustering and how the results inform educator interventions."**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Why Clustering for Risk Identification [1 mark]**

Regression predicts a **continuous score**; clustering identifies **discrete risk groups**. Educators need actionable categories ("at-risk", "average", "high-performer"), not just a continuous number. k-Means partitions students into $k$ homogeneous groups based on feature similarity.

**Step 2: Choosing k — The Elbow Method [2 marks]**

Compute the **Within-Cluster Sum of Squares (WCSS)** for $k = 1, 2, \ldots, 10$:

$$\text{WCSS}(k) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$$

Plot WCSS vs $k$. The "elbow" point — where the rate of WCSS decrease sharply drops — is the optimal $k$. For student data, this is typically at $k = 3$ (high/average/at-risk).

**Mathematical interpretation**: The elbow point maximizes the **marginal information gain** per additional cluster.

**Step 3: Validation via Silhouette Analysis [2 marks]**

For each data point $i$, compute the silhouette coefficient:

$$s(i) = \frac{b(i) - a(i)}{\max\{a(i), b(i)\}}$$

where:
- $a(i) = \frac{1}{\vert C_{I(i)} \vert - 1} \sum_{j \in C_{I(i)}, j \neq i} d(i, j)$ (mean intra-cluster distance)
- $b(i) = \min_{J \neq I(i)} \frac{1}{\vert C_J \vert} \sum_{j \in C_J} d(i, j)$ (mean nearest-cluster distance)

**Interpretation**: $s(i) \in [-1, +1]$
- $s(i) \approx +1$: Well-clustered (far from neighboring clusters)
- $s(i) \approx 0$: On the boundary between two clusters
- $s(i) \approx -1$: Possibly assigned to the wrong cluster

The optimal $k$ **maximizes the mean silhouette score** $\bar{s} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} s(i)$.

**Step 4: Clustering Evaluation Metrics [1 mark]**

| Metric | Formula | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **Silhouette Score** | $\bar{s} = \frac{1}{N}\sum s(i)$ | Higher is better; range $[-1, +1]$ |
| **Davies-Bouldin Index** | $\text{DB} = \frac{1}{k}\sum_{i=1}^{k} \max_{j \neq i} \left(\frac{\sigma_i + \sigma_j}{d(c_i, c_j)}\right)$ | Lower is better; measures cluster compactness |
| **Inertia (WCSS)** | $\text{WCSS} = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$ | Lower is better; used in Elbow Method |

**Step 5: Educator Intervention Framework [1 mark]**

| Cluster | Profile | Intervention |
|---|---|---|
| **High Performer** | Internal avg > 40, Attendance > 85% | Mentor weaker students; advanced projects |
| **Average Performer** | Internal avg 25–40, Attendance 75–85% | Monitor; encourage consistency |
| **At-Risk** | Internal avg < 25, Attendance < 75% | Mandatory counseling; remedial classes; parental contact |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1**: Students apply k-Means **without scaling the features**. Since internal marks are in $[0, 50]$ and attendance is in $[0, 100]$, the Euclidean distance will be dominated by attendance. **Always apply StandardScaler before k-Means.**
> - **Common Mistake 2**: Confusing the **Elbow Method** (uses WCSS) with the **Silhouette Method** (uses silhouette coefficient). They are complementary, not interchangeable.
> - **Common Mistake 3**: Students forget to **interpret the clusters** after computing them. The k-Means algorithm assigns labels (0, 1, 2), but the student must **map** these to meaningful categories (at-risk, average, high) by analyzing cluster centroids.
> - **Common Mistake 4**: Writing "use DBSCAN or hierarchical clustering" without justification. While valid alternatives, the question specifically asks for k-Means — straying from the requirement loses marks.
> - **Common Mistake 5**: Forgetting that clustering is **unsupervised** — there is no "ground truth" label. Evaluation must use internal validity indices (Silhouette, Davies-Bouldin), not accuracy.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Read this 5 minutes before the exam)**

### ✅ Core Concepts to Memorize
- [ ] **Six stages** of the universal ML project pipeline: Problem → Data → EDA → Pre-processing → Model → Evaluation.
- [ ] **MNIST characteristics**: 70,000 images, $28 \times 28$ grayscale, 10 classes, balanced.
- [ ] **Project 2 features**: Internal marks (3), attendance, assignments, lab marks, final exam score.
- [ ] **Why ensembles work**: Bias-variance decomposition — bagging reduces variance, boosting reduces bias, stacking learns optimal combination.
- [ ] **k-Means algorithm**: Assignment step + Update step, iterated until convergence.
- [ ] **Elbow Method** uses WCSS; **Silhouette Analysis** uses $s(i) = (b(i) - a(i)) / \max(a(i), b(i))$.

### ✅ Formulas That MUST Be on Your Finger-tips
- [ ] Accuracy = $(TP + TN) / (TP + TN + FP + FN)$
- [ ] Precision = $TP / (TP + FP)$
- [ ] Recall = $TP / (TP + FN)$
- [ ] F1-Score = $2 \cdot P \cdot R / (P + R)$
- [ ] RMSE = $\sqrt{\frac{1}{n}\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}$
- [ ] R² = $1 - \frac{\sum(y_i - \hat{y}_i)^2}{\sum(y_i - \bar{y})^2}$
- [ ] WCSS = $\sum_{i=1}^{k}\sum_{x \in C_i} \vert\vert x - \mu_i \vert\vert^2$
- [ ] Silhouette: $s(i) = (b(i) - a(i))/\max(a(i), b(i))$

### ✅ Algorithm Selection Decision Tree
- **Image data** → PCA → k-NN / SVM / Random Forest → Voting Ensemble
- **Tabular regression** → Linear Regression / Random Forest / Gradient Boosting → Stacking
- **Risk segmentation** → StandardScaler → k-Means (k=3) → Silhouette validation

### ✅ Common Pitfalls to AVOID
- ❌ Reporting only accuracy for classification (always include Precision, Recall, F1).
- ❌ Reporting only R² for regression (always include RMSE and MAE).
- ❌ Applying k-Means without feature scaling.
- ❌ Forgetting to perform train-test split before training.
- ❌ Claiming "ensemble is always better" without numerical evidence.
- ❌ Using accuracy on imbalanced data without checking class distribution.
- ❌ Ignoring outlier detection in regression projects.
- ❌ Confusing classification accuracy with regression R².
- ❌ Writing Python code in theory exams (use pseudocode or flow diagrams instead).

### ✅ KTU-Specific Exam Strategy
1. **Always state the problem type first** (classification / regression / clustering).
2. **Always justify algorithm choice** with one technical reason.
3. **Always report multiple metrics**, not just one.
4. **Always mention cross-validation** when discussing model evaluation.
5. **Always include a diagram** (pipeline flowchart) for full marks in 14-mark questions.
6. **For Project 2, always address BOTH regression AND clustering** — examiners specifically look for this hybrid treatment.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Note:** The two sample projects in the KTU 2024 syllabus are **exemplars**, not mandatory topics. Students may identify **any real-life problem** and apply the same six-stage pipeline. The examiner's primary evaluation criterion is the **rigor of methodology**, not the choice of problem. A well-executed project on **movie recommendation** or **weather prediction** will score equally well as MNIST or student performance — provided all six stages are demonstrably present.
